Reproducibility: not applicable. for the requested feature: current main lacks `claude-cli-interactive`, and the PR body plus proof branch show a real Windows gateway path for the new backend. The review finding is source-reproducible from the new provider-id registration and the unchanged legacy-only Claude history importer.
commands.review_followup: This PR needs author repair for backend parity plus explicit maintainer security/product approval; the remaining decision is not a safe autonomous cleanup close.

target.title: feat(anthropic): claude-cli-interactive backend — stream reasoning via local TLS proxy (experimental)
target.body:
## Summary

- **Problem.** OpenClaw's `claude-cli` backend invokes `claude -p --output-format stream-json` (headless). Starting **June 15, 2026** that path moves onto Anthropic's separate programmatic credit pool ([VentureBeat coverage](https://venturebeat.com/technology/anthropic-reinstates-openclaw-and-third-party-agent-usage-on-claude-subscriptions-with-a-catch)) — fixed monthly allowance, billed at API rates, doesn't roll over.
- **Why it matters.** Every OpenClaw Opus turn on a Claude subscription will drain that fixed credit post–June 15 instead of counting against the interactive subscription limits subscribers are paying for. Opus turns exhaust the credit fast.
- **What changed.** New opt-in `claude-cli-interactive` backend. Each turn spawns one fresh `bun wrapper.ts …` that boots a loopback HTTPS MITM on a 127.0.0.1 port, taps every API SSE event, re-emits the events as `claude -p --output-format stream-json` JSONL records, and kills the claude child on `end_turn`. A `tty-spoof.cjs` `NODE_OPTIONS=--require` preload keeps claude in interactive mode under piped stdio.
- **What did NOT change (scope boundary).** Existing `claude-cli` backend untouched. `agents.defaults.cliBackends.claude-cli` user overrides inherit into the interactive backend via `inheritUserConfigFrom` with `-p`-mode flags stripped. System CA store is not mutated (`NODE_EXTRA_CA_CERTS` scoped to the spawned claude child only). Non–`api.anthropic.com` CONNECT tunnels pass through unmodified. Tool-use rendering / waiting-on-tool injection lands in #82285, not here.
- **AI-assisted:** yes — drafted with Claude.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed:** `claude` running under OpenClaw in interactive (subscription) mode with full `init` / `stream_event*` / `result` JSONL reconstruction on the wrapper's stdout, sourced from the loopback MITM proxy's SSE tap.
- **Real environment tested:** Windows 11 Pro 26200, Node v24.12.0, pnpm, Bun, `@anthropic-ai/claude-code` v2.1.142 (npm-installed), Opus 4.7 (`claude-opus-4-7`), live OpenClaw gateway driving the backend per-turn over Telegram.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** `unset NODE_OPTIONS && pnpm exec vitest run extensions/anthropic/cli-backend-interactive.test.ts`; `pnpm exec tsc --noEmit -p extensions/anthropic/tsconfig.json`; set `cliBackends.claude-cli-interactive` active in user config; restart the OpenClaw gateway; send a Telegram message that triggers an Opus 4.7 turn; observe wrapper boot in stderr, SSE flow through `127.0.0.1:<port>`, JSONL arriving at OpenClaw; send a follow-up message with `--resume <sessionId>` to verify resume.
- **Evidence after fix:**
  - vitest: `Test Files 1 passed (1) / Tests 5 passed (5)` — `normalizeConfig` branches (inherited claude-cli match, direct Bun-path override, env passthrough, no-override skip).
  - Gateway log excerpt + cert listing + active-config snapshot on the [`proof-assets/pr-81851`](https://github.com/anagnorisis2peripeteia/openclaw/tree/proof-assets/pr-81851/.proof) branch. Embedded sample (one of three real turns in the gateway-log excerpt):

    ```
    cli exec: provider=claude-cli-interactive model=opus useResume=true session=present
    cli argv: bun <NPM>\…\wrapper.ts --effort max --verbose --setting-sources user --resume <SESSION> --allowedTools mcp__openclaw__* --model opus <prompt:N chars>
    [openclaw-proxy] certs ready: <HOME>\.openclaw\proxy-certs\ca.crt
    [openclaw-proxy] MITM proxy active on 127.0.0.1:17081 — Anthropic API traffic intercepted locally to capture thinking_delta events
    [openclaw-proxy] spawning claude binary: <NPM>\…\@anthropic-ai\claude-code\bin\claude.exe
    [openclaw-proxy] result flushed, waiting for session write before kill
    [openclaw-proxy] session file updated, killing claude
    ```
- **Observed result after fix:** per-turn lifecycle terminates cleanly — wrapper emits `init`, a continuous stream of `stream_event` records, then a single `result` on `end_turn`, sets `intentionalKill = true`, and `claude.kill()`s the child so the per-turn process tree exits 0.
- **What was not tested:** Linux/macOS (developed on Windows 11 only); bundled-MCP `claude-config-file` mode end-to-end with multiple MCP servers active (only `mcp__openclaw__*` exercised live); forced Anthropic-side `429` / `overloaded_error` mid-stream (the `api_error` synthetic event path is implemented but I did not provoke a real 429).

## Change Type

- [x] Feature

## Scope

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Integrations

## Linked

- Related: #81550 (programmatic-mode reasoning bridge — this PR is the interactive-mode equivalent on a separate backend); #82285 (tool-use injection, builds on the `stream_event` surface this PR establishes).

## Root Cause

N/A — feature PR. The lack of thinking visibility on subscription is a property of `claude -p` (headless) mode, not a regression in OpenClaw.

## Regression Test Plan

- **Target:** `extensions/anthropic/cli-backend-interactive.test.ts`.
- **Scenario locked in:** `normalizeConfig` post-merge shape across four branches — inherited claude-cli match (forces `command="bun"`, stashes binary on env), direct Bun-path override (keeps the override as the spawn command, no env stash), env passthrough preservation, no-override case (env passthrough omitted so wrapper falls back to PATH lookup of `"claude"`).
- **Why smallest reliable guardrail:** the failure mode is config-shape drift inside `normalizeConfig` — fully exercised synchronously without spawning bun or claude, runs in ms vs the minutes an e2e run would cost.

## Risks and Mitigations

- **Risk:** newly-installed user with no inherited `claude-cli` override could spawn `bun` as the claude binary if `normalizeConfig` stashed `merged.command` indiscriminately.
  - **Mitigation:** `normalizeConfig` consults `context.config.agents.defaults.cliBackends.claude-cli.command`; only stashes the env passthrough on a match. Regression test asserts the env var is `undefined` for the no-override branch.
- **Risk:** `tty-spoof.cjs` is a runtime support file (not a build output) and could be omitted from packaged installs.
  - **Mitigation:** declared in `extensions/anthropic/package.json#openclaw.build.staticAssets`; postbuild asset copier ships it alongside the wrapper.
- **Risk:** winget-installed `claude.exe` shadows the npm install via PATHEXT on Windows.
  - **Mitigation:** wrapper spawns `OPENCLAW_INTERACTIVE_CLAUDE_BINARY` directly when the parent backend resolved a binary, never falling back to a bare PATH lookup.
- **Risk:** title-gen / Haiku side-requests inject `{"title":"…"}` artifacts into the reasoning stream.
  - **Mitigation:** mitm-server classifies each `/v1/messages` request into `auxiliary` (no tools) / `compaction` (compact.ts prompt markers) / `tool_followup` (last msg has tool_result) / `normal`. Wrapper drops auxiliary entirely, streams compaction as `thinking_delta`, emits a result only on `normal` `end_turn`. Response-content fingerprint backup (`<analysis>` + Primary-Request-and-Intent + Pending-Tasks) catches anything that slipped past the request-level classifier.
- **Risk:** spilled-prompt overflow file leaks to concurrent runs via shared tempdir + `--add-dir`.
  - **Mitigation:** per-run subdir under `${tmpdir()}/openclaw-interactive-proxy/run-<32-hex-bytes>/` (0700), file 0600, `--add-dir` scoped to the per-run subdir only.
- **Risk:** local CA + leaf key persisted under user's OpenClaw config dir could be reused if an attacker gets read access.
  - **Mitigation:** trust scoped to the spawned claude child via `NODE_EXTRA_CA_CERTS` only (no system trust mutation); CA issues solely the one `api.anthropic.com` leaf the per-turn proxy needs; key files hardened to 0600 with fail-closed regeneration; maintainer security review invited.
- **Risk:** Anthropic-side non-SSE 4xx/5xx on `/v1/messages` (429, billing, overloaded) hangs the wrapper because claude doesn't self-exit on API error.
  - **Mitigation:** mitm-server emits a synthetic `api_error` event for non-SSE 4xx/5xx; wrapper surfaces a `result` with `is_error: true` and exits nonzero so the CLI runner treats the turn as failed and triggers failover.
- **Risk:** two resumes of the same Claude session race on the same transcript.
  - **Mitigation:** backend config sets `serialize: true` so the framework queues same-session runs; different sessions still run concurrently. mitm-server binds both proxy stages on port 0 so concurrent proxies can't collide on a preferred port either.

## Security Impact

- **New permissions/capabilities:** Yes — per-turn local TLS MITM on a loopback port. Scoped to the spawned claude child via `NODE_EXTRA_CA_CERTS`; system trust store untouched. Local CA + leaf key cached under user's OpenClaw config dir at 0700/0600.
- **Secrets/tokens handling changed:** No — wrapper does not read, log, or modify the Anthropic auth token; it is forwarded byte-for-byte to the re-issued upstream request.
- **New/changed network calls:** Yes — claude's outbound HTTPS to `api.anthropic.com:443` is rerouted via `HTTPS_PROXY=http://127.0.0.1:<port>` inside the child. Non–`api.anthropic.com` CONNECT tunnels (statsig, datadog, etc.) bypass the TLS terminator entirely.
- **Command/tool execution surface changed:** No — same `claude` binary, same allowed-tools (`mcp__openclaw__*`), same session semantics.
- **Data access scope changed:** No — proxy sees only the SSE bytes claude itself sees on the wire; nothing exfiltrated or aggregated outside the per-turn process.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible: existing `claude-cli` backend unchanged. Opt-in via the `claude-cli-interactive` id; no default flips.
- New optional env: `OPENCLAW_INTERACTIVE_PROXY_DEBUG=1` enables verbose wrapper stderr.
- Migration needed: no.

## Prior art

[`smithersai/claude-p`](https://github.com/smithersai/claude-p) runs `claude` without `-p` but bypasses the JSONL streaming surface entirely — works for the raw final text but loses the `stream_event` records that reasoning and tool-use rendering depend on. This PR reproduces the `-p --output-format stream-json` shape record-for-record from the SSE tap so existing OpenClaw stream consumers layer on unchanged.

commands.review_summary:
The PR adds SOUL-aware Codex personality routing/config, moves Codex context-engine additions into turn reference context, adjusts lightweight project-doc handling, updates Codex docs, and adds focused tests.

Reproducibility: yes. Source inspection shows /codex resume writes bindings without personality, while the PR clears those bindings when SOUL makes params.personality resolve to none; I did not execute tests because this review is read-only.
commands.review_followup: A narrow repair can preserve manual/legacy Codex bindings and add focused regression coverage without reopening the broader SOUL personality policy.

target.title: [codex] Route native personality through SOUL context
target.body:
## Why The previous fix correctly stopped raw OpenClaw workspace prompt files from being promoted into Codex developer instructions. That avoided giving user-editable files developer priority. However, it left an important edge case: if Codex's native personality overlay remained active, a custom OpenClaw `SOUL.md` could still lose personality priority to Codex's built-in friendly/pragmatic personality. That is especially awkward because OpenClaw is not only a coding agent surface, while the Codex harness still needs to preserve its coding-capable anchor and native tool discipline. ## Summary This PR keeps the Codex harness anchor intact while preventing Codex's named native personality overlay from outranking a user-provided OpenClaw `SOUL.md`. It combines five related pieces in one reviewable change: 1. **SOUL-aware Codex personality routing** - Adds `plugins.entries.codex.config.personalityMode`. - Defaults to `"soul_when_present"`. - When OpenClaw injects `SOUL.md` into the Codex turn context, OpenClaw sends Codex app-server `personality: "none"` for that thread. - This is a structured app-server enum, not prompt text that tells the model to "have no personality". The positive personality source remains `SOUL.md`. 2. **A narrow OpenClaw/Codex bridge** - Keeps Codex base/model/project-doc behavior in the native Codex lane. - Adds developer-level bridge wording only when OpenClaw turn context is injected. - Explains that Codex is the native execution harness, OpenClaw is the host runtime, and OpenClaw turn context is reference data rather than developer policy. - When the resolved personality mode disables Codex's native personality, explicitly points personality/tone/agent voice at the SOUL file when compatible with higher-priority safety, privacy, tool, and current-request rules. 3. **Prompt hardening plus docs/tests** - Keeps raw `SOUL.md`, skills, and bootstrap files out of Codex developer instructions. - Projects context-engine `systemPromptAddition` into Codex turn reference context instead of Codex developer instructions. - Documents the priority model, configuration modes, and context-engine behavior. - Adds regression tests for default SOUL behavior, opt-out behavior, bridge wording, session binding, context-engine projection, and type/config parsing. 4. **Lightweight Codex AGENTS.md fix** - Keeps lightweight cron user input byte-for-byte. - Stops sending `project_doc_max_bytes: 0` for lightweight native Codex threads. - This stops OpenClaw from explicitly suppressing Codex's native project-doc loader. Actual `AGENTS.md` ingestion remains Codex app-server behavior. 5. **CI test hardening** - Keeps `source-delivery-plan.test.ts` focused on source-delivery matching by mocking the outbound target normalizer. - Avoids loading the full channel plugin registry in a tiny unit test, which was timing out in the unrelated `checks-node-core-fast` shard. This PR chooses the middle path: - Keep Codex's native harness identity. - Do not promote raw SOUL text to developer instructions. - Disable Codex's named native personality only when SOUL is present by default. - Use a bridge instruction to say where personality should come from. - Leave explicit config escape hatches for deployments that want Codex personality always on or always off. ## Prior PR Context The preceding prompt-surface work was directionally correct: it separated PI, CLI, ACP, subagent, and Codex app-server prompt lanes so Codex-native turns no longer inherited PI wording, global command hints, or OpenClaw GPT-5 behavior overlays by accident. It also moved OpenClaw workspace bootstrap files out of Codex `developerInstructions`, which avoided the worse failure mode of giving user-editable files developer priority. The adversarial review found that the previous fix created a more subtle priority gap: - Raw `SOUL.md` was no longer over-promoted into developer instructions. - But Codex's own named personality overlay could still sit above the SOUL turn context. - That means a user-provided OpenClaw personality could be present but lose to the native Codex friendly/pragmatic personality. - The bridge existed conceptually, but the PR body did not describe its shape, why it was needed, or why it was preferable to promoting SOUL. This PR is the follow-up that closes that gap while preserving the good part of the previous PR: Codex owns its native harness identity; OpenClaw owns runtime routing and context interpretation; user-editable OpenClaw files remain reference context. ## Bridge Text Shape This PR labels and tests the bridge as a developer-level **OpenClaw/Codex turn-context bridge**. It is intentionally small and has three parts: 1. **Host/runtime disambiguation:** OpenClaw is the host runtime; Codex is the native execution harness. Codex's base identity describes harness capability and tool discipline, not a promise that every OpenClaw turn is coding work. 2. **Authority boundary:** OpenClaw may provide files, memory, skills, and channel context as turn-input reference data. That context helps with task framing and style, but raw files and skills are not developer policy. 3. **SOUL pointer when Codex personality is disabled:** If the resolved personality mode disables Codex's native personality and `SOUL.md` is in the OpenClaw turn context, use it for personality, tone, and agent voice when compatible with higher-priority safety, privacy, tool, and current-request rules. If `personalityMode: "codex"` is configured, `SOUL.md` remains reference context and this personality pointer is omitted. The bridge is developer-level because it is an instruction about **how to rank and interpret** lower-priority OpenClaw context. The SOUL file itself stays lower priority because it is user/workspace-editable content. ## Why Not Promote SOUL We considered the main alternatives: | Option | Result | | --- | --- | | Put raw `SOUL.md` back into `developerInstructions` | Rejected. It would make user-editable personality text developer policy and undo the safety win from the previous PR. | | Leave Codex native personality on and rely on SOUL in turn context | Rejected as the default. It leaves the custom OpenClaw personality below Codex's named friendly/pragmatic overlay, so conflicts resolve in the wrong direction. | | Always disable Codex native personality | Supported via `personalityMode: "none"`, but not the default because some deployments intentionally want stock Codex personality. | | Disable Codex native personality only when SOUL is present, then bridge to SOUL | Chosen. It removes the competing named overlay only when a positive OpenClaw personality source exists. | | Keep Codex native personality even with SOUL | Supported via `personalityMode: "codex"` for operators who prefer Codex's built-in style stack. | The important detail is that `personality: "none"` is not prompt text. It is structured Codex app-server configuration. The model is not told "you have no personality"; instead, the named native overlay is omitted and, when SOUL is the intended personality source, the bridge points to `SOUL.md`. ## Overrides Kept This PR intentionally keeps the native Codex overrides that are actually runtime policy: | Override | Why it stays | | --- | --- | | Visible reply routing for `sourceReplyDeliveryMode === "message_tool_only"` | This is channel/session delivery policy. Automatic mode remains neutral and uses the active Codex delivery path. | | Codex `spawn_agent` primary for Codex subagents | Native Codex subagents should stay native. OpenClaw `sessions_spawn` remains reserved for OpenClaw/ACP delegation. | | Codex base/model instructions | OpenClaw should not override native Codex `baseInstructions`. This PR only uses `developerInstructions` for OpenClaw runtime policy and bridge text. | ## Prompt Priority Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Codex base/model instructions
harness identity, tools, safety"] --> B["Codex native personality enum"] B --> C["OpenClaw developer bridge
runtime + context authority"] C --> D["OpenClaw turn context
SOUL, IDENTITY, USER, MEMORY, skills"] D --> E["Current user request"] S["SOUL.md injected
and personality override active"] --> N["Send app-server personality: none"] N --> B S --> C C --> P["Use SOUL.md for personality/tone
only when Codex personality is disabled"] ``` Important distinction: `personality: "none"` is sent as Codex app-server configuration. It is not inserted into the model prompt as "you have no personality." When the default SOUL path disables Codex's native personality, the model receives a positive developer bridge that names `SOUL.md` as the personality/tone source. ## Context-Engine Hardening Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR CE["context-engine systemPromptAddition"] --> CTX["Codex turn reference context"] CTX --> INPUT["turn/start input"] CE -. "no longer for native Codex" .-> DEV["Codex developerInstructions"] ``` For OpenClaw-assembled prompt surfaces, context engines can still contribute to the assembled system prompt. For native Codex app-server turns, that dynamic guidance is now quoted as turn reference context so retrieval/memory hints do not outrank Codex system/developer policy. ## Configuration New Codex plugin config: ```json5 { "plugins": { "entries": { "codex": { "config": { "personalityMode": "soul_when_present" } } } } } ``` Accepted values: | Value | Behavior | | --- | --- | | `"soul_when_present"` | Default. Disable Codex's named native personality only when OpenClaw injects `SOUL.md`; use SOUL as the personality/tone reference. | | `"codex"` | Keep Codex's native personality overlay even when `SOUL.md` is present. | | `"none"` | Always send Codex `personality: "none"` for app-server threads. | ## Real behavior proof **Behavior addressed:** Native Codex app-server turns now keep Codex harness identity while routing personality priority to OpenClaw `SOUL.md` when SOUL is injected. Context-engine `systemPromptAddition` is no longer appended to Codex developer instructions, and lightweight Codex turns no longer send `project_doc_max_bytes: 0`. **Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw source checkout on macOS, built through the real OpenClaw CLI/runtime build path at `/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/openclaw-prompt-surface-runtime-separation`, commit `d6626baffd`. **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** ```bash pnpm openclaw --help node --input-type=module --eval ' import { readdirSync, readFileSync } from "node:fs"; const findDistChunk = (needle) => readdirSync("./dist").find((name) => name.endsWith(".js") && readFileSync(`./dist/${name}`, "utf8").includes(needle) ); const configFile = findDistChunk("function resolveCodexPersonalityOverride"); const lifecycleFile = findDistChunk("function buildDeveloperInstructions"); const { s: readCodexPluginConfig, u: resolveCodexPersonalityOverride } = await import(`./dist/${configFile}`); const { i: buildDeveloperInstructions, _: projectContextEngineAssemblyForCodex, o: buildThreadStartParams, } = await import(`./dist/${lifecycleFile}`); const defaultDeveloperInstructions = buildDeveloperInstructions( { sourceReplyDeliveryMode: "automatic" }, { includeOpenClawTurnContextBridge: true, useSoulPromptContextForPersonality: true }, ); const codexModeDeveloperInstructions = buildDeveloperInstructions( { sourceReplyDeliveryMode: "automatic" }, { includeOpenClawTurnContextBridge: true, useSoulPromptContextForPersonality: false }, ); const projection = projectContextEngineAssemblyForCodex({ assembledMessages: [], originalHistoryMessages: [], prompt: "hello from real smoke", systemPromptAddition: "context-engine system", systemPromptAdditionMode: "context", }); const lightweightStart = buildThreadStartParams( { provider: "openai", modelId: "gpt-5.4-codex", authProfileStore: { version: 1, profiles: {} }, bootstrapContextMode: "lightweight", bootstrapContextRunKind: "cron", sourceReplyDeliveryMode: "automatic", }, { cwd: process.cwd(), dynamicTools: [], appServer: { approvalPolicy: "on-request", approvalsReviewer: "user", sandbox: "workspace-write", }, developerInstructions: "lightweight proof", }, ); console.log(JSON.stringify({ openclawCheckout: process.cwd(), commit: "d6626baffd", distChunks: { configFile, lifecycleFile }, defaultSoulPersonality: resolveCodexPersonalityOverride({ pluginConfig: readCodexPluginConfig({}), hasSoulPromptContext: true, }), explicitNonePersonality: resolveCodexPersonalityOverride({ pluginConfig: readCodexPluginConfig({ personalityMode: "none" }), hasSoulPromptContext: false, }), codexModePersonality: resolveCodexPersonalityOverride({ pluginConfig: readCodexPluginConfig({ personalityMode: "codex" }), hasSoulPromptContext: true, }) ?? null, defaultBridgeMentionsHostRuntime: defaultDeveloperInstructions.includes("OpenClaw is the host runtime"), defaultBridgePointsToSoul: defaultDeveloperInstructions.includes("use SOUL.md from the OpenClaw turn context"), codexModeBridgeMentionsHostRuntime: codexModeDeveloperInstructions.includes("OpenClaw is the host runtime"), codexModeBridgePointsToSoul: codexModeDeveloperInstructions.includes("use SOUL.md from the OpenClaw turn context"), rawSoulTextInDeveloperInstructions: defaultDeveloperInstructions.includes("Soul voice goes here."), contextEngineDeveloperAddition: projection.developerInstructionAddition ?? null, contextEnginePromptHasReferenceHeader: projection.promptText.includes("Context-engine guidance:"), contextEnginePromptHasSystemAddition: projection.promptText.includes("context-engine system"), lightweightProjectDocMaxBytes: lightweightStart.config?.project_doc_max_bytes ?? null, lightweightProjectDocSuppressesAgents: lightweightStart.config?.project_doc_max_bytes === 0, }, null, 2)); ' ``` **Evidence after fix:** Copied live terminal output from the built OpenClaw checkout: ```text OpenClaw 2026.5.17 (d6626ba) — All your chats, one OpenClaw. { "openclawCheckout": "/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/openclaw-prompt-surface-runtime-separation", "commit": "d6626baffd", "distChunks": { "configFile": "config-Om76xpjW.js", "lifecycleFile": "thread-lifecycle-CYnqtUYN.js" }, "defaultSoulPersonality": "none", "explicitNonePersonality": "none", "codexModePersonality": null, "defaultBridgeMentionsHostRuntime": true, "defaultBridgePointsToSoul": true, "codexModeBridgeMentionsHostRuntime": true, "codexModeBridgePointsToSoul": false, "rawSoulTextInDeveloperInstructions": false, "contextEngineDeveloperAddition": null, "contextEnginePromptHasReferenceHeader": true, "contextEnginePromptHasSystemAddition": true, "lightweightProjectDocMaxBytes": null, "lightweightProjectDocSuppressesAgents": false } ``` **Observed result after fix:** The real built OpenClaw checkout reports the default SOUL path resolves to Codex `personality: "none"`, the `"codex"` config path leaves native personality unset, developer bridge text is present and points to SOUL only for the default SOUL override path, raw SOUL contents are absent from developer instructions, context-engine guidance is present in turn reference context while developer-instruction addition is `null`, and the lightweight Codex path does not suppress native project-doc loading by sending `project_doc_max_bytes: 0`. **What was not tested:** No live paid model request or remote Codex app-server network turn was run for this PR. The live proof exercises the built OpenClaw runtime/prompt-construction path locally; request-construction behavior is covered by focused app-server tests. ## Behavior Addressed Before: - Raw `SOUL.md` was not in developer instructions, which was good. - But Codex's named native personality overlay could still remain active above the SOUL turn context. - Context-engine `systemPromptAddition` could be appended to Codex developer instructions. - Lightweight Codex turns sent `project_doc_max_bytes: 0`, which suppressed native `AGENTS.md` loading while the OpenClaw-side bootstrap was also skipped. After: - Raw SOUL text remains turn context, not developer policy. - The developer bridge says how to interpret OpenClaw context and when to use SOUL for voice. - Default Codex personality is neutralized only when SOUL is present, while `personalityMode: "codex"` keeps the native personality overlay and omits the SOUL personality pointer. - Context-engine dynamic additions are turn reference context for native Codex. - Matching `thread_bootstrap` resumes keep current-turn context-engine guidance as reference context without reprojecting old bootstrap history. - Lightweight Codex turns preserve exact user input without disabling native Codex project docs. ## Real Environment Tested Checkout: `/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/openclaw-prompt-surface-runtime-separation` Branch: `codex/soul-personality-bridge` Commit: `d6626baffd [codex] route native personality through soul context` ## Exact Steps Or Commands Run After This Patch ```bash git diff --check OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs \ extensions/codex/src/app-server/config.test.ts \ extensions/codex/src/app-server/thread-lifecycle.test.ts \ extensions/codex/src/app-server/session-binding.test.ts \ extensions/codex/src/app-server/context-engine-projection.test.ts \ extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.context-engine.test.ts \ extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.test.ts \ src/infra/outbound/source-delivery-plan.test.ts pnpm check:test-types ``` ## Evidence After Fix - Focused Vitest: `7 passed (7)` files, `295 passed (295)` tests. - Source delivery plan test is included in the focused Vitest command; local duration for that file after hardening was `279ms` in the pre-rebase run. - Type test check: `pnpm check:test-types` passed. - Whitespace check: `git diff --check` passed. ## Observed Result After Fix - Codex app-server thread start includes `personality: "none"` when SOUL is injected under the default config. - `personalityMode: "codex"` leaves native Codex personality unset even when SOUL is present. - Developer instructions contain the OpenClaw/Codex bridge and no raw SOUL contents; `personalityMode: "codex"` omits the SOUL personality pointer. - Cron/lightweight bootstrap path does not add the OpenClaw context bridge or personality override, and no longer disables native Codex project docs. - Context-engine `systemPromptAddition` is absent from Codex developer instructions and present in turn reference context, including matching `thread_bootstrap` resume turns. ## What Was Not Tested - No full local repo-wide suite was run; this was intentionally kept to focused local validation plus type checks under the Lexar resource policy. - No live Codex app-server manual smoke was run in this PR turn; the change is covered by the app-server request-construction and run-attempt tests. ## Adversarial Findings Addressed | Severity | Finding | Disposition in this PR | | --- | --- | --- | | 9/10 | Lightweight Codex turns could suppress native `AGENTS.md` while OpenClaw text said Codex loads it. The bad chain was `project_doc_max_bytes: 0` plus skipped OpenClaw bootstrap context. | Fixed. Lightweight cron turns still keep exact user input, and OpenClaw no longer sends the project-doc suppression override. Actual `AGENTS.md` ingestion remains Codex app-server behavior. | | 8/10 | OpenClaw broad behavior/personality overlays can duplicate Codex's native base/model/personality stack. | Kept narrow. Native Codex still avoids the OpenClaw GPT-5 behavior contract in `developerInstructions`; this PR handles the remaining personality conflict with app-server `personalityMode`. | | 7/10 | OpenClaw skills/context can double with Codex native project context or accidentally gain developer priority. | Kept as reference context. Raw workspace files, skills, and context-engine additions stay out of Codex developer policy; the bridge tells Codex how to interpret them. | | 7/10 | Some non-PI prompt surfaces can still receive OpenClaw workflow hints when tools are nonempty. | Not widened here. That belongs to the OpenClaw assembled-prompt surface, not the native Codex app-server lane. This PR keeps only Codex-specific subagent disambiguation in the Codex lane. | ##Adversarial Audit Addressed v2 After the first green run, a fresh read-only audit found two real merge-blocking precision issues and one proof-language overclaim: | Finding | Fix in this PR | | --- | --- | | `personalityMode: "codex"` kept Codex's native personality unset, but the bridge still told the model to use `SOUL.md` for personality. | The SOUL personality pointer now depends on the resolved app-server personality override, not raw SOUL presence. In `"codex"` mode, `SOUL.md` stays turn-input reference context and the bridge does not point personality at it. | | Matching `thread_bootstrap` context-engine resumes skipped old bootstrap history, but also dropped the current turn's `systemPromptAddition`. | Matching bootstrap resumes now omit the old bootstrap transcript while still projecting the current turn's `systemPromptAddition` as quoted reference context, not developer policy. | | The PR proof phrasing implied live proof of Codex actually loading `AGENTS.md`. | The body now says exactly what this PR proves: OpenClaw no longer sends `project_doc_max_bytes: 0`, so it no longer suppresses Codex's native project-doc loader. |

target.title: Add inbound_observed hook for skipped inbound messages
target.body:
## Summary

Adds a read-only `inbound_observed` plugin hook for passive inbound visibility, then emits it from Telegram when a group message is skipped by `requireMention`/mention gating.

This gives archive/audit/history plugins a supported way to observe messages that are visible to the gateway but intentionally do not enter an agent session or trigger a reply.

## Why

`message_received` currently represents messages that have entered the later dispatch/session path. Moving it earlier would risk changing existing plugin semantics. A dedicated observational hook keeps real-time session routing lean while still enabling full-history archive plugins to record skipped inbound messages.

The new hook is intentionally narrow:

- read-only event payload
- fire-and-forget execution
- no session creation
- no reply/routing override
- includes `skipped` and `skipReason` metadata for mention-gated drops

## Validation

- `pnpm vitest run src/plugins/wired-hooks-message.test.ts extensions/telegram/src/bot-message-context.body.test.ts src/hooks/message-hook-mappers.test.ts extensions/telegram/src/bot-message-context.group-body.test.ts`
- `pnpm build`
- `pnpm check:test-types`

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed:** Telegram group messages skipped by `requireMention` were invisible to passive archive/history plugins because they returned before the normal dispatch/session hook path. This patch adds a read-only `inbound_observed` hook and emits it for those skipped Telegram group messages.

**Real environment tested:** Live OpenClaw server on the maintainer's local Mac mini, using the local patched source checkout and the existing `conversation-archive` plugin.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:**

```text
scripts/source-switch-local.sh
scripts/local-status.sh
scripts/check-conversation-archive.sh --hours 24
node --check /Users/cindy/.openclaw/extensions/conversation-archive/index.js
```

I also ran a plugin-level smoke that invoked `conversation-archive` through `inbound_observed` and wrote a raw archive JSONL entry.

**Evidence after fix:**

Representative redacted live output from the patched server:

```text
OpenClaw status
service check
  launchd: system/ai.openclaw.gateway.headless running
  listener: 17185 (port 18789)

Channels
  Telegram ON OK
  BlueBubbles ON OK
  FoloToy ON OK
  @larksuite/openclaw-lark ON OK

Telegram skipped-group inbound_observed archive hook patch is present
workspace archive directory exists and is writable
workspace latest archive sample includes required fields
workspace-caoz latest archive sample includes required fields
workspace-food-group latest archive sample includes required fields
workspace-wife latest archive sample includes required fields

Conversation archive health: OK
```

The plugin-level smoke wrote a JSONL archive entry with:

```text
source: inbound-observed
chat_type: group
text: quiet group message
```

**Observed result after fix:** A skipped Telegram group message can now reach the passive archive plugin via `inbound_observed` without creating an agent session or triggering a reply, and the archive health check still passes on the live setup.

**What was not tested:** I did not send a real test message into a production Telegram group to avoid posting noise. The live proof used the deployed runtime, archive health checks, and a plugin-level smoke for the new hook path.

target.title: feat(telegram): add guest mode
target.body: ## Summary - Problem: OpenClaw did not handle Telegram Guest Bot `guest_message` updates, so bots could not answer `@bot` guest queries from groups where the bot is not a member. - Why it matters: Guest Bots are a Telegram-supported way to let a bot answer from outside a group without joining it, but they require a different update type and response API than normal group messages. - What changed: Telegram now accepts `guest_message` updates, routes them through an isolated direct-style agent turn, preserves sender metadata, and delivers exactly one visible response through `answerGuestQuery`. - What did NOT change (scope boundary): Normal Telegram group/direct message handling remains on the existing send path. Guest Mode does not inherit per-group policies or normal group conversation bindings; it uses account-level Telegram allowlist rules. ## Change Type (select all) - [ ] Bug fix - [x] Feature - [ ] Refactor required for the fix - [x] Docs - [ ] Security hardening - [ ] Chore/infra ## Scope (select all touched areas) - [x] Gateway / orchestration - [ ] Skills / tool execution - [ ] Auth / tokens - [ ] Memory / storage - [x] Integrations - [x] API / contracts - [x] UI / DX - [ ] CI/CD / infra ## Linked Issue/PR - Related #79077 - Partially addresses #79077 - [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression ## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs) - Behavior or issue addressed: Telegram bots can answer Guest Bot queries from group chats where the bot is not a member when `channels.telegram.guest.enabled` is enabled or auto-detected from Telegram bot metadata. - Real environment tested: Ubuntu Linux, local OpenClaw source checkout, systemd user gateway, Telegram channel configured, OpenAI Codex with GPT-5.5. - Exact steps or command run after this patch: 1. Activate the guest mode in the BotFather telegram bot settings. 2. Built OpenClaw from this branch. 3. Built Control UI assets. 4. Restarted the local gateway. 5. Sent a Telegram guest query to the bot from an external group where the bot was not a member. (`@openclawbot what time is it, can you read previous messages here? What is my telegram id, what is this channel / group id?`) 6. Verified that one visible answer appeared in Telegram. 7. Verified that users not allowed by the account-level allowlist could not use Guest Mode. 8. Tested groups where the bot is already a member to check that normal Telegram group handling did not regress. - Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output): - Observed result after fix: The guest query created an isolated direct-style Telegram guest session and produced one visible Telegram answer. Unauthorized guest senders were blocked by account-level allowFrom. Normal groups where the bot is already a member continued to work through the existing Telegram message path. - What was not tested: Bot-to-bot mode, long-running multi-account (more than 3) production soak testing, and cross-platform release-package install proof. - Before evidence (optional but encouraged): Before this patch, OpenClaw did not include guest_message in Telegram allowed updates and had no answerGuestQuery delivery path, so Guest Bot queries were not converted into agent turns. (So this did just not exist previously) ## Root Cause (if applicable) For bug fixes or regressions, explain why this happened, not just what changed. Otherwise write `N/A`. If the cause is unclear, write `Unknown`. - Root cause: N/A for a new feature. Telegram Guest Bots use a new update and response shape that the existing Telegram channel did not support. - Missing detection / guardrail: Existing Telegram tests covered normal message updates, but not guest_message allowed updates, guest session isolation, or answerGuestQuery delivery. - Contributing context (if known): Guest Mode is similar to inline behavior from a product perspective, but Telegram delivers it as a guest message that must be answered through the Bot API guest-answer endpoint rather than the normal send-message path. ## Regression Test Plan (if applicable) For bug fixes or regressions, name the smallest reliable test coverage that should catch this. Otherwise write `N/A`. - Coverage level that should have caught this: - [x] Unit test - [x] Seam / integration test - [ ] End-to-end test - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient - Target test or file: - extensions/telegram/src/bot.create-telegram-bot.test.ts - extensions/telegram/src/allowed-updates.test.ts - extensions/telegram/src/probe.test.ts - extensions/telegram/src/webhook.test.ts - Scenario the test should lock in: A guest update is accepted, routed to an isolated guest session, keeps sender metadata available to the agent, does not inherit normal group bindings or group policies, and sends only one visible Telegram guest answer. - Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: These tests exercise the Telegram plugin boundary where allowed updates, bot capability detection, guest routing, session context, authorization, and delivery behavior meet, without requiring live Telegram credentials. - Existing test that already covers this (if any): N/A. - If no new test is added, why not: N/A. New tests were added. ## User-visible / Behavior Changes Telegram Guest Mode can now be enabled with channels.telegram.guest.enabled. - true: handle Telegram guest queries. - false: ignore Telegram guest queries. - auto: handle guest queries when Telegram bot metadata reports guest-query support. Guest Mode uses the Telegram account-level allowlist. target.title: fix(feishu): share webhook server across accounts on same host:port
target.body:
When multiple Feishu accounts use webhook mode on the same host:port, each previously created its own http.Server, causing EADDRINUSE on the second listen().

Add a server pool keyed by host:port in monitor.state.ts.  Accounts joining an existing pool entry register a route (token + handler); the pool dispatcher routes by verification token or app_id.

Single-account fast-path: when only one route is registered the dispatcher delegates directly with zero body-parsing overhead.

Cleanup in stopFeishuMonitorState correctly removes routes and only closes the server when the last account leaves.

Files changed: monitor.transport.ts, monitor.state.ts

## Summary

- **Problem:** `monitorWebhook()` in `monitor.transport.ts` calls `http.createServer()` per account. When ≥2 accounts share the same `host:port` (or rely on the default port 3000), the second `server.listen()` fails with `EADDRINUSE`.
- **Why it matters:** Multi-account Feishu/Lark webhook deployments on a single OpenClaw instance are impossible without manual per-account port assignment plus an external reverse proxy — unnecessarily complex for a common use case.
- **What changed:** Introduced a `webhookServerPool` (`Map<string, WebhookServerEntry>`) in `monitor.state.ts` keyed by `host:port`. `monitorWebhook()` checks the pool before creating a new server; subsequent accounts join the existing entry. A pool dispatcher routes incoming requests by `header.token` or `header.app_id`. Body size is capped at `FEISHU_WEBHOOK_MAX_BODY_BYTES` (1 MB) before buffering. Existing rate-limit and body-guard logic is extracted into `createGuardedHandler()` (no behavioral change). `stopFeishuMonitorState()` is updated with pool-aware cleanup.
- **What did NOT change (scope boundary):** WebSocket mode, `monitor.ts`, `monitor.account.ts`, `monitor.startup.ts`, event dispatching internals, config schema, and all other files remain untouched.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor
- [ ] Docs
- [x] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related #

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

None — single-account setups hit the fast path and behave identically. Multi-account setups that previously crashed with `EADDRINUSE` now work without config changes.

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? `No`
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? `No` — verification tokens are read from existing config, used only for in-process routing comparison, never logged or transmitted.
- New/changed network calls? `No` — the same `http.createServer()` and `server.listen()` calls are used; the change is that they are shared instead of duplicated.
- Command/tool execution surface changed? `No`
- Data access scope changed? `No`
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: N/A

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux (tested on Ubuntu 22.04)
- Runtime/container: Node.js 20+
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel: Feishu / Lark International (webhook mode)
- Relevant config (redacted):
  ```yaml
  channels:
    feishu:
      accounts:
        bot-a:
          appId: "cli_aaa..."
          verificationToken: "tok_aaa..."
          connectionMode: webhook
        bot-b:
          appId: "cli_bbb..."
          verificationToken: "tok_bbb..."
          connectionMode: webhook
      webhookPort: 3000
  ```

### Steps

1. Configure 2+ Feishu accounts in webhook mode sharing the same port (or relying on default 3000).
2. Start the OpenClaw gateway.
3. Observe the second account starts successfully and joins the shared server.
4. Send a webhook event with `header.token` matching bot-b's verification token.
5. Verify bot-b's event dispatcher receives the event.

### Expected

- All accounts share a single HTTP server on port 3000.
- Each account's events are routed by verification token.
- Stopping a single account removes its route; server stays alive for remaining accounts.
- Stopping all accounts (or the last one) closes the server.

### Actual

- Before fix: second account crashes with `Error: listen EADDRINUSE: address already in use 0.0.0.0:3000`.
- After fix: all accounts share the server and route correctly.

## Evidence

- Code review of `monitor.transport.ts` and `monitor.state.ts` diffs.
- `oxfmt --check` passes (formatting compliant).

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios: multi-account webhook startup (no EADDRINUSE), single-account fast-path (zero overhead), account removal with pool cleanup.
- Edge cases checked: oversized body (413 Payload Too Large), malformed JSON body (404 Not Found), `url_verification` challenge forwarded to first handler, abort signal on creator vs joiner account, `appId` fallback routing when `verificationToken` is absent.
- What you did **not** verify: full integration test with Lark SDK's `adaptDefault()` consuming the replayed `Readable` stream end-to-end (would require a live Feishu app).

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? `Yes`
- Config/env changes? `No`
- Migration needed? `No`
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A

## Failure Recovery (if this breaks)

- How to disable/revert this change quickly: assign unique `webhookPort` per account in config to bypass the shared server path entirely.
- Files/config to restore: revert `monitor.transport.ts` and `monitor.state.ts` to upstream/main.
- Known bad symptoms reviewers should watch for: webhook events delivered to wrong account (token mismatch), 404 responses for valid webhooks (routing failure), memory growth from unbounded body buffering (mitigated by 1 MB cap).

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: Replayed `Readable` stream may behave differently from the original `IncomingMessage` for edge-case middleware in the Lark SDK.
  - Mitigation: `replayRequest` copies `method`, `url`, `headers`, `rawHeaders`, `httpVersion`, `socket`, and `connection` from the original request. The single-account fast path avoids replay entirely.
- Risk: Multi-account body buffering adds latency vs. direct streaming.
  - Mitigation: Feishu webhook payloads are typically <10 KB. The 1 MB cap (`FEISHU_WEBHOOK_MAX_BODY_BYTES`) prevents memory abuse. Single-account setups are unaffected (fast path).

target.title: fix(heartbeat): do not append workspace path hint to user-configured prompts
target.body:
Commit 604f22c introduced `appendHeartbeatWorkspacePathHint` to help agents find the correct HEARTBEAT.md when using the built-in default prompt. The hint was appended whenever the prompt mentioned 'heartbeat.md', regardless of whether it was the default or a user-supplied custom prompt.

This breaks user-configured prompts: the appended text may contradict the user's intent, and users who explicitly exclude HEARTBEAT.md references from their prompt cannot prevent the hint from being injected.

Fix: detect whether the heartbeat prompt came from the user's config (non-empty custom string) vs the built-in default, and skip the hint injection for user-authored prompts. Exec/cron event prompts are always generated internally and are never treated as user prompts.

Adds a focused unit test covering the custom-prompt guard, the default-prompt append path, and the no-duplicate guard.

Fixes #40255.

---
AI-assisted (Claude). Fully tested and reviewed.

target.title: feat(feishu): support index parameter for doc insert action
target.body:
## Problem

The `feishu_doc(action: "insert")` tool requires `after_block_id` as a mandatory parameter. This makes it impossible to insert content at the very beginning of a document — there is no block to reference when you want to prepend content before the first block.

This is a common need when building documents in reverse-chronological order (e.g., daily diaries with newest entries first).

## Solution

- Make `after_block_id` optional
- Add an optional `index` parameter (0-based position among document root children, default: 0 = beginning)
- When `after_block_id` is provided, behavior is exactly the same as before (fully backward compatible)
- When `after_block_id` is omitted, use `index` to determine insert position

The underlying Feishu Descendant API (`documentBlockDescendant.create`) already supports the `index` parameter. This change simply exposes it through the tool schema.

## Changes

- `extensions/feishu/src/doc-schema.ts`: `after_block_id` → optional, added `index` parameter
- `extensions/feishu/src/docx.ts`: `insertDoc` function handles both modes; call site passes `p.index`

## Testing

Tested locally on a live Feishu document:
- `feishu_doc(action: "insert", index: 0, ...)` — successfully inserts at document beginning ✅
- `feishu_doc(action: "insert", after_block_id: "xxx", ...)` — existing behavior unchanged ✅

target.title: Feature(memory-lancedb): Agent Scoping #38797
target.body:
## Summary

- **Problem:** The `memory-lancedb` plugin shared a single global memory pool across all agents. An agent could recall memories captured by a different agent, and explicit tool calls (`memory_recall`, `memory_store`, `memory_forget`) had no way to scope reads/writes to the calling agent.
- **Why it matters:** In multi-agent setups this causes cross-contamination: agent A sees agent B's memories, and agent B can overwrite or forget agent A's entries via tool calls
- **What changed:** Added an opt-in `agentScoping` config boolean. When enabled, memories are tagged with the originating `agentId` at capture time; recall and tool calls filter to that agent's memories plus legacy globally-shared memories (`agentId = ""`). A best-effort migration adds the `agentId` column to existing LanceDB tables. Tool registration switched from static objects to factories so each agent-run gets its own scoped context.
- **What did NOT change:** Default behavior (`agentScoping === false`) is fully backward-compatible — the global memory pool is preserved and no existing data is altered.

## Change Type

- [ ] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [x] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #38797

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- New config option `agentScoping` (boolean, default `false`) in the `memory-lancedb` plugin settings.
- When `agentScoping === true`:
  - Auto-captured memories are tagged with the originating agent's ID.
  - Auto-recall and all memory tools (`memory_recall`, `memory_store`, `memory_forget`) only see memories belonging to the current agent plus legacy untagged (globally shared) memories.
- When `agentScoping === false` (default): no behavior change.

## Security Impact

- New permissions/capabilities? No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? No
- New/changed network calls? No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? No
- Data access scope changed? `es
  - **Risk:** When `agentScoping === true`, agents cannot read each other's scoped memories. The `agentId` value passed through `ctx.agentId` is used in a SQL filter string. A malformed `agentId` could be used for SQL injection against the LanceDB filter.
  - **Mitigation:** `agentId` is validated against `/^[a-zA-Z0-9_\-.:@]{0,128}$/` before interpolation; values that do not match are rejected, not silently stripped.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux (WSL2)
- Runtime/container: Node 22 / Bun
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel: memory-lancedb extension
- Relevant config: `agentScoping === true` in plugin config

### Steps

1. Enable `agentScoping === true` in the `memory-lancedb` plugin config.
2. Run two agents with different `agentId` values and store a memory from each.
3. Recall from each agent. Each agent should only see its own memories and untagged legacy entries.

### Expected

- Agent A's recall returns only Agent A's memories and global memories.
- Agent B's recall returns only Agent B's memories and global memories.

### Actual

- Before this change: both agents see all memories.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after

```
BEFORE:
FAIL extensions/memory-lancedb/index.test.ts > memory plugin e2e > passes configured dimensions to OpenAI embeddings API
TypeError: recallTool.execute is not a function

AFTER:
✓ extensions/memory-lancedb/index.test.ts > memory plugin e2e (12 passed, 1 skipped)
```

## Human Verification

- Verified scenarios: all unit tests pass; `agentScoping: false` path exercises existing code unchanged; migration skips gracefully when `agentId` column already present.
- Edge cases checked: legacy tables without `agentId` column get it added with `''` default on first open; migration failure is caught and does not crash the plugin.
- What you did **not** verify: live multi-agent end-to-end with a real LanceDB instance and real agent IDs.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? Yes — `agentScoping` defaults to `false`; existing behavior unchanged.
- Config/env changes? Yes — new optional `agentScoping` boolean in plugin config.
- Migration needed? No — existing LanceDB tables are migrated automatically on first open; existing memories are preserved as globally-shared by setting `agentId` empty string.

## Failure Recovery

- How to disable/revert this change quickly: set `agentScoping` to false in plugin config.
- Files/config to restore: `extensions/memory-lancedb/config.ts`, `extensions/memory-lancedb/index.ts`.
- Known bad symptoms reviewers should watch for: memories not appearing in recall after enabling `agentScoping` (likely the `agentId` is not being propagated through `ctx` in that hook context).

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: `agentId` injection via `ctx.agentId` into the LanceDB SQL filter string.
  - Mitigation: strict regex allowlist (`/^[a-zA-Z0-9_\-.:@]{0,128}$/`) rejects unexpected values before interpolation.
- Risk: Migration (`addColumns`) fails silently on unusual LanceDB versions, leaving the table without the `agentId` column. Scoped filtering is then skipped.
  - Mitigation: best-effort catch with logger warning; plugin continues to function in unscoped mode.

target.title: fix(cron): filter reasoning/thinking payloads from announce delivery
target.body:
## Summary

- Filters reasoning/thinking payloads from cron announce delivery pipeline end-to-end
- Propagates `isReasoning` flag through `buildEmbeddedRunPayloads` `.map()` return and `EmbeddedPiRunResult` type
- Filters reasoning in all 3 payload-selection helpers + `firstText` fallback + error-recovery detection
- Reasoning payloads are **never** deliverable and **never** mask error status

### Root cause

The `isReasoning` flag was correctly set internally by `buildEmbeddedRunPayloads()` but:
1. Not propagated in the `.map()` return value (`payloads.ts`)
2. Not declared on the `EmbeddedPiRunResult` payload type (`types.ts`)
3. Not filtered in cron delivery helpers (`helpers.ts`)
4. Not filtered in `firstText` fallback (`run.ts:754`)
5. Not excluded from error-recovery detection (`run.ts:844`)

### Commits

target.title: fix: auto-reset session on surrogate encoding error to prevent infinite loop
target.body:
## Problem

When conversation history contains unpaired Unicode surrogate characters (e.g. lone \\uD800\-\\uDFFF\), JSON serialization of the LLM request body fails with:

\\\
LLM request rejected: The request body is not valid JSON: no low surrogate in string
\\\

Previously, this error was surfaced as a chat reply. That reply then got appended to the conversation context, causing every subsequent request to also fail — creating an **infinite loop** that required a manual \/new\ or \/reset\ to escape.

## Fix

- **\isSurrogateEncodingError()\** — new detector in \errors.ts\ matching \
ot valid json\ + \surrogate\ patterns
- **\ ormatAssistantErrorText()\** — returns a friendly user-facing message instead of the raw API error
- **\^Ggent-runner-execution.ts\** — auto-resets the session transcript when a surrogate error is detected, following the same pattern used for role-ordering and Gemini corruption errors

## Observed in production

A session accumulated surrogate characters in its history (likely from tool output or file content). Every message — including the error reply itself — re-triggered the failure, looping indefinitely until manually reset.

target.title: fix(telegram): apply reply_to only to first stream preview to avoid 'Deleted message'
target.body:
## Summary

- **Problem:** During Telegram streaming, the reply header briefly shows "Deleted message" when tool boundaries cause `forceNewMessage()` to create new preview messages.
- **Root cause:** After `forceNewMessage()`, the new preview message was also sent with `reply_to_message_id` pointing to the user's original message. When the first preview was archived and deleted, Telegram briefly showed "Deleted message" for subsequent previews that also tried to reply to the same user message.
- **Fix:** Track whether `reply_to_message_id` has been applied with `hasAppliedReply` flag. Only the first preview message gets the reply context; subsequent messages after `forceNewMessage()` are sent without a reply target.

## Change Type

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations (Telegram)
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #39718
- Related to #32924 (which fixes reasoning lane deletions, different root cause)

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- The first assistant streaming reply in Telegram will still quote/reply to the user's message
- Subsequent assistant messages in the same stream (e.g., after tool use) will NOT quote/reply to the user's message
- This prevents the brief "Deleted message" artifact that appeared during streaming

## Security Impact

- New permissions/capabilities? No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? No
- New/changed network calls? No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? No
- Data access scope changed? No

## Repro + Verification

### Steps

1. Enable streaming in Telegram
2. Ask the agent a question that triggers tool use (multiple response segments)
3. **Before fix:** Brief "Deleted message" appears in reply header during streaming
4. **After fix:** First message replies to user, subsequent messages have no reply context

### Evidence

- [x] New test: `applies reply_to_message_id only to the first message, not after forceNewMessage (#39718)`
- [x] All 28 tests in `draft-stream.test.ts` pass

## Human Verification

- Verified: First message gets reply_to, subsequent messages do not
- Edge cases: Materialize still uses reply context correctly for the permanent message
- What I did not verify: Live Telegram testing (no Telegram access)

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? Yes (slightly changed UX for multi-segment responses)
- Config/env changes? No
- Migration needed? No

## Failure Recovery

- How to revert: Revert PR commit
- Known bad symptoms: Users might miss the reply context on subsequent messages (acceptable trade-off)

target.title: fix: drain stdout/stderr before resolving child wait
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes #30711 — stdout lost when a backgrounded sandbox process produces block-buffered output that is only flushed at exit.

- When a sandboxed process (e.g. `bun` on a pipe inside Docker) uses block-buffered stdout, all output is flushed at exit. The child `close` event and the final stdout data event can land in the same libuv poll cycle, causing the promise microtask chain to snapshot `session.aggregated` before the data callback runs — silently losing the output.
- Added a `setImmediate` yield in the child adapter's `wait()` after the `close` event resolves, ensuring pending I/O callbacks (stdout/stderr data events) are processed before the promise chain continues.
- Added a second `setImmediate` yield in the supervisor before calling `adapter.dispose()`, as a safety net for data callbacks enqueued between the adapter yield and the supervisor's snapshot.

## Changes

| File | Change |
|------|--------|
| `src/process/supervisor/adapters/child.ts` | `setImmediate` yield after `child.once("close")` resolves |
| `src/process/supervisor/supervisor.ts` | `setImmediate` yield before `adapter.dispose()` |
| `src/agents/bash-tools.exec.background-stdout.test.ts` | 3 regression tests |

## Test plan

- [x] New test: backgrounded process stdout captured when output flushed at exit
- [x] New test: backgrounded process captures both stdout and stderr
- [x] New test: large block-buffered output captured correctly
- [x] All 44 existing exec tests pass (no regression)
- [x] All 24 supervisor tests pass
- [x] All 5 process registry tests pass
- [x] All 10 process tool tests pass
- [ ] Manual: run a bun/node command that takes >10s in sandbox, verify `process log` shows output without `2>&1`
