target.title: [codex] Route native personality through SOUL context
target.body:
## Why The previous fix correctly stopped raw OpenClaw workspace prompt files from being promoted into Codex developer instructions. That avoided giving user-editable files developer priority. However, it left an important edge case: if Codex's native personality overlay remained active, a custom OpenClaw `SOUL.md` could still lose personality priority to Codex's built-in friendly/pragmatic personality. That is especially awkward because OpenClaw is not only a coding agent surface, while the Codex harness still needs to preserve its coding-capable anchor and native tool discipline. ## Summary This PR keeps the Codex harness anchor intact while preventing Codex's named native personality overlay from outranking a user-provided OpenClaw `SOUL.md`. It combines five related pieces in one reviewable change: 1. **SOUL-aware Codex personality routing** - Adds `plugins.entries.codex.config.personalityMode`. - Defaults to `"soul_when_present"`. - When OpenClaw injects `SOUL.md` into the Codex turn context, OpenClaw sends Codex app-server `personality: "none"` for that thread. - This is a structured app-server enum, not prompt text that tells the model to "have no personality". The positive personality source remains `SOUL.md`. 2. **A narrow OpenClaw/Codex bridge** - Keeps Codex base/model/project-doc behavior in the native Codex lane. - Adds developer-level bridge wording only when OpenClaw turn context is injected. - Explains that Codex is the native execution harness, OpenClaw is the host runtime, and OpenClaw turn context is reference data rather than developer policy. - When the resolved personality mode disables Codex's native personality, explicitly points personality/tone/agent voice at the SOUL file when compatible with higher-priority safety, privacy, tool, and current-request rules. 3. **Prompt hardening plus docs/tests** - Keeps raw `SOUL.md`, skills, and bootstrap files out of Codex developer instructions. - Projects context-engine `systemPromptAddition` into Codex turn reference context instead of Codex developer instructions. - Documents the priority model, configuration modes, and context-engine behavior. - Adds regression tests for default SOUL behavior, opt-out behavior, bridge wording, session binding, context-engine projection, and type/config parsing. 4. **Lightweight Codex AGENTS.md fix** - Keeps lightweight cron user input byte-for-byte. - Stops sending `project_doc_max_bytes: 0` for lightweight native Codex threads. - This stops OpenClaw from explicitly suppressing Codex's native project-doc loader. Actual `AGENTS.md` ingestion remains Codex app-server behavior. 5. **CI test hardening** - Keeps `source-delivery-plan.test.ts` focused on source-delivery matching by mocking the outbound target normalizer. - Avoids loading the full channel plugin registry in a tiny unit test, which was timing out in the unrelated `checks-node-core-fast` shard. This PR chooses the middle path: - Keep Codex's native harness identity. - Do not promote raw SOUL text to developer instructions. - Disable Codex's named native personality only when SOUL is present by default. - Use a bridge instruction to say where personality should come from. - Leave explicit config escape hatches for deployments that want Codex personality always on or always off. ## Prior PR Context The preceding prompt-surface work was directionally correct: it separated PI, CLI, ACP, subagent, and Codex app-server prompt lanes so Codex-native turns no longer inherited PI wording, global command hints, or OpenClaw GPT-5 behavior overlays by accident. It also moved OpenClaw workspace bootstrap files out of Codex `developerInstructions`, which avoided the worse failure mode of giving user-editable files developer priority. The adversarial review found that the previous fix created a more subtle priority gap: - Raw `SOUL.md` was no longer over-promoted into developer instructions. - But Codex's own named personality overlay could still sit above the SOUL turn context. - That means a user-provided OpenClaw personality could be present but lose to the native Codex friendly/pragmatic personality. - The bridge existed conceptually, but the PR body did not describe its shape, why it was needed, or why it was preferable to promoting SOUL. This PR is the follow-up that closes that gap while preserving the good part of the previous PR: Codex owns its native harness identity; OpenClaw owns runtime routing and context interpretation; user-editable OpenClaw files remain reference context. ## Bridge Text Shape This PR labels and tests the bridge as a developer-level **OpenClaw/Codex turn-context bridge**. It is intentionally small and has three parts: 1. **Host/runtime disambiguation:** OpenClaw is the host runtime; Codex is the native execution harness. Codex's base identity describes harness capability and tool discipline, not a promise that every OpenClaw turn is coding work. 2. **Authority boundary:** OpenClaw may provide files, memory, skills, and channel context as turn-input reference data. That context helps with task framing and style, but raw files and skills are not developer policy. 3. **SOUL pointer when Codex personality is disabled:** If the resolved personality mode disables Codex's native personality and `SOUL.md` is in the OpenClaw turn context, use it for personality, tone, and agent voice when compatible with higher-priority safety, privacy, tool, and current-request rules. If `personalityMode: "codex"` is configured, `SOUL.md` remains reference context and this personality pointer is omitted. The bridge is developer-level because it is an instruction about **how to rank and interpret** lower-priority OpenClaw context. The SOUL file itself stays lower priority because it is user/workspace-editable content. ## Why Not Promote SOUL We considered the main alternatives: | Option | Result | | --- | --- | | Put raw `SOUL.md` back into `developerInstructions` | Rejected. It would make user-editable personality text developer policy and undo the safety win from the previous PR. | | Leave Codex native personality on and rely on SOUL in turn context | Rejected as the default. It leaves the custom OpenClaw personality below Codex's named friendly/pragmatic overlay, so conflicts resolve in the wrong direction. | | Always disable Codex native personality | Supported via `personalityMode: "none"`, but not the default because some deployments intentionally want stock Codex personality. | | Disable Codex native personality only when SOUL is present, then bridge to SOUL | Chosen. It removes the competing named overlay only when a positive OpenClaw personality source exists. | | Keep Codex native personality even with SOUL | Supported via `personalityMode: "codex"` for operators who prefer Codex's built-in style stack. | The important detail is that `personality: "none"` is not prompt text. It is structured Codex app-server configuration. The model is not told "you have no personality"; instead, the named native overlay is omitted and, when SOUL is the intended personality source, the bridge points to `SOUL.md`. ## Overrides Kept This PR intentionally keeps the native Codex overrides that are actually runtime policy: | Override | Why it stays | | --- | --- | | Visible reply routing for `sourceReplyDeliveryMode === "message_tool_only"` | This is channel/session delivery policy. Automatic mode remains neutral and uses the active Codex delivery path. | | Codex `spawn_agent` primary for Codex subagents | Native Codex subagents should stay native. OpenClaw `sessions_spawn` remains reserved for OpenClaw/ACP delegation. | | Codex base/model instructions | OpenClaw should not override native Codex `baseInstructions`. This PR only uses `developerInstructions` for OpenClaw runtime policy and bridge text. | ## Prompt Priority Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Codex base/model instructions
harness identity, tools, safety"] --> B["Codex native personality enum"] B --> C["OpenClaw developer bridge
runtime + context authority"] C --> D["OpenClaw turn context
SOUL, IDENTITY, USER, MEMORY, skills"] D --> E["Current user request"] S["SOUL.md injected
and personality override active"] --> N["Send app-server personality: none"] N --> B S --> C C --> P["Use SOUL.md for personality/tone
only when Codex personality is disabled"] ``` Important distinction: `personality: "none"` is sent as Codex app-server configuration. It is not inserted into the model prompt as "you have no personality." When the default SOUL path disables Codex's native personality, the model receives a positive developer bridge that names `SOUL.md` as the personality/tone source. ## Context-Engine Hardening Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR CE["context-engine systemPromptAddition"] --> CTX["Codex turn reference context"] CTX --> INPUT["turn/start input"] CE -. "no longer for native Codex" .-> DEV["Codex developerInstructions"] ``` For OpenClaw-assembled prompt surfaces, context engines can still contribute to the assembled system prompt. For native Codex app-server turns, that dynamic guidance is now quoted as turn reference context so retrieval/memory hints do not outrank Codex system/developer policy. ## Configuration New Codex plugin config: ```json5 { "plugins": { "entries": { "codex": { "config": { "personalityMode": "soul_when_present" } } } } } ``` Accepted values: | Value | Behavior | | --- | --- | | `"soul_when_present"` | Default. Disable Codex's named native personality only when OpenClaw injects `SOUL.md`; use SOUL as the personality/tone reference. | | `"codex"` | Keep Codex's native personality overlay even when `SOUL.md` is present. | | `"none"` | Always send Codex `personality: "none"` for app-server threads. | ## Real behavior proof **Behavior addressed:** Native Codex app-server turns now keep Codex harness identity while routing personality priority to OpenClaw `SOUL.md` when SOUL is injected. Context-engine `systemPromptAddition` is no longer appended to Codex developer instructions, and lightweight Codex turns no longer send `project_doc_max_bytes: 0`. **Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw source checkout on macOS, built through the real OpenClaw CLI/runtime build path at `/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/openclaw-prompt-surface-runtime-separation`, commit `d6626baffd`. **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** ```bash pnpm openclaw --help node --input-type=module --eval ' import { readdirSync, readFileSync } from "node:fs"; const findDistChunk = (needle) => readdirSync("./dist").find((name) => name.endsWith(".js") && readFileSync(`./dist/${name}`, "utf8").includes(needle) ); const configFile = findDistChunk("function resolveCodexPersonalityOverride"); const lifecycleFile = findDistChunk("function buildDeveloperInstructions"); const { s: readCodexPluginConfig, u: resolveCodexPersonalityOverride } = await import(`./dist/${configFile}`); const { i: buildDeveloperInstructions, _: projectContextEngineAssemblyForCodex, o: buildThreadStartParams, } = await import(`./dist/${lifecycleFile}`); const defaultDeveloperInstructions = buildDeveloperInstructions( { sourceReplyDeliveryMode: "automatic" }, { includeOpenClawTurnContextBridge: true, useSoulPromptContextForPersonality: true }, ); const codexModeDeveloperInstructions = buildDeveloperInstructions( { sourceReplyDeliveryMode: "automatic" }, { includeOpenClawTurnContextBridge: true, useSoulPromptContextForPersonality: false }, ); const projection = projectContextEngineAssemblyForCodex({ assembledMessages: [], originalHistoryMessages: [], prompt: "hello from real smoke", systemPromptAddition: "context-engine system", systemPromptAdditionMode: "context", }); const lightweightStart = buildThreadStartParams( { provider: "openai", modelId: "gpt-5.4-codex", authProfileStore: { version: 1, profiles: {} }, bootstrapContextMode: "lightweight", bootstrapContextRunKind: "cron", sourceReplyDeliveryMode: "automatic", }, { cwd: process.cwd(), dynamicTools: [], appServer: { approvalPolicy: "on-request", approvalsReviewer: "user", sandbox: "workspace-write", }, developerInstructions: "lightweight proof", }, ); console.log(JSON.stringify({ openclawCheckout: process.cwd(), commit: "d6626baffd", distChunks: { configFile, lifecycleFile }, defaultSoulPersonality: resolveCodexPersonalityOverride({ pluginConfig: readCodexPluginConfig({}), hasSoulPromptContext: true, }), explicitNonePersonality: resolveCodexPersonalityOverride({ pluginConfig: readCodexPluginConfig({ personalityMode: "none" }), hasSoulPromptContext: false, }), codexModePersonality: resolveCodexPersonalityOverride({ pluginConfig: readCodexPluginConfig({ personalityMode: "codex" }), hasSoulPromptContext: true, }) ?? null, defaultBridgeMentionsHostRuntime: defaultDeveloperInstructions.includes("OpenClaw is the host runtime"), defaultBridgePointsToSoul: defaultDeveloperInstructions.includes("use SOUL.md from the OpenClaw turn context"), codexModeBridgeMentionsHostRuntime: codexModeDeveloperInstructions.includes("OpenClaw is the host runtime"), codexModeBridgePointsToSoul: codexModeDeveloperInstructions.includes("use SOUL.md from the OpenClaw turn context"), rawSoulTextInDeveloperInstructions: defaultDeveloperInstructions.includes("Soul voice goes here."), contextEngineDeveloperAddition: projection.developerInstructionAddition ?? null, contextEnginePromptHasReferenceHeader: projection.promptText.includes("Context-engine guidance:"), contextEnginePromptHasSystemAddition: projection.promptText.includes("context-engine system"), lightweightProjectDocMaxBytes: lightweightStart.config?.project_doc_max_bytes ?? null, lightweightProjectDocSuppressesAgents: lightweightStart.config?.project_doc_max_bytes === 0, }, null, 2)); ' ``` **Evidence after fix:** Copied live terminal output from the built OpenClaw checkout: ```text OpenClaw 2026.5.17 (d6626ba) — All your chats, one OpenClaw. { "openclawCheckout": "/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/openclaw-prompt-surface-runtime-separation", "commit": "d6626baffd", "distChunks": { "configFile": "config-Om76xpjW.js", "lifecycleFile": "thread-lifecycle-CYnqtUYN.js" }, "defaultSoulPersonality": "none", "explicitNonePersonality": "none", "codexModePersonality": null, "defaultBridgeMentionsHostRuntime": true, "defaultBridgePointsToSoul": true, "codexModeBridgeMentionsHostRuntime": true, "codexModeBridgePointsToSoul": false, "rawSoulTextInDeveloperInstructions": false, "contextEngineDeveloperAddition": null, "contextEnginePromptHasReferenceHeader": true, "contextEnginePromptHasSystemAddition": true, "lightweightProjectDocMaxBytes": null, "lightweightProjectDocSuppressesAgents": false } ``` **Observed result after fix:** The real built OpenClaw checkout reports the default SOUL path resolves to Codex `personality: "none"`, the `"codex"` config path leaves native personality unset, developer bridge text is present and points to SOUL only for the default SOUL override path, raw SOUL contents are absent from developer instructions, context-engine guidance is present in turn reference context while developer-instruction addition is `null`, and the lightweight Codex path does not suppress native project-doc loading by sending `project_doc_max_bytes: 0`. **What was not tested:** No live paid model request or remote Codex app-server network turn was run for this PR. The live proof exercises the built OpenClaw runtime/prompt-construction path locally; request-construction behavior is covered by focused app-server tests. ## Behavior Addressed Before: - Raw `SOUL.md` was not in developer instructions, which was good. - But Codex's named native personality overlay could still remain active above the SOUL turn context. - Context-engine `systemPromptAddition` could be appended to Codex developer instructions. - Lightweight Codex turns sent `project_doc_max_bytes: 0`, which suppressed native `AGENTS.md` loading while the OpenClaw-side bootstrap was also skipped. After: - Raw SOUL text remains turn context, not developer policy. - The developer bridge says how to interpret OpenClaw context and when to use SOUL for voice. - Default Codex personality is neutralized only when SOUL is present, while `personalityMode: "codex"` keeps the native personality overlay and omits the SOUL personality pointer. - Context-engine dynamic additions are turn reference context for native Codex. - Matching `thread_bootstrap` resumes keep current-turn context-engine guidance as reference context without reprojecting old bootstrap history. - Lightweight Codex turns preserve exact user input without disabling native Codex project docs. ## Real Environment Tested Checkout: `/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/openclaw-prompt-surface-runtime-separation` Branch: `codex/soul-personality-bridge` Commit: `d6626baffd [codex] route native personality through soul context` ## Exact Steps Or Commands Run After This Patch ```bash git diff --check OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs \ extensions/codex/src/app-server/config.test.ts \ extensions/codex/src/app-server/thread-lifecycle.test.ts \ extensions/codex/src/app-server/session-binding.test.ts \ extensions/codex/src/app-server/context-engine-projection.test.ts \ extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.context-engine.test.ts \ extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.test.ts \ src/infra/outbound/source-delivery-plan.test.ts pnpm check:test-types ``` ## Evidence After Fix - Focused Vitest: `7 passed (7)` files, `295 passed (295)` tests. - Source delivery plan test is included in the focused Vitest command; local duration for that file after hardening was `279ms` in the pre-rebase run. - Type test check: `pnpm check:test-types` passed. - Whitespace check: `git diff --check` passed. ## Observed Result After Fix - Codex app-server thread start includes `personality: "none"` when SOUL is injected under the default config. - `personalityMode: "codex"` leaves native Codex personality unset even when SOUL is present. - Developer instructions contain the OpenClaw/Codex bridge and no raw SOUL contents; `personalityMode: "codex"` omits the SOUL personality pointer. - Cron/lightweight bootstrap path does not add the OpenClaw context bridge or personality override, and no longer disables native Codex project docs. - Context-engine `systemPromptAddition` is absent from Codex developer instructions and present in turn reference context, including matching `thread_bootstrap` resume turns. ## What Was Not Tested - No full local repo-wide suite was run; this was intentionally kept to focused local validation plus type checks under the Lexar resource policy. - No live Codex app-server manual smoke was run in this PR turn; the change is covered by the app-server request-construction and run-attempt tests. ## Adversarial Findings Addressed | Severity | Finding | Disposition in this PR | | --- | --- | --- | | 9/10 | Lightweight Codex turns could suppress native `AGENTS.md` while OpenClaw text said Codex loads it. The bad chain was `project_doc_max_bytes: 0` plus skipped OpenClaw bootstrap context. | Fixed. Lightweight cron turns still keep exact user input, and OpenClaw no longer sends the project-doc suppression override. Actual `AGENTS.md` ingestion remains Codex app-server behavior. | | 8/10 | OpenClaw broad behavior/personality overlays can duplicate Codex's native base/model/personality stack. | Kept narrow. Native Codex still avoids the OpenClaw GPT-5 behavior contract in `developerInstructions`; this PR handles the remaining personality conflict with app-server `personalityMode`. | | 7/10 | OpenClaw skills/context can double with Codex native project context or accidentally gain developer priority. | Kept as reference context. Raw workspace files, skills, and context-engine additions stay out of Codex developer policy; the bridge tells Codex how to interpret them. | | 7/10 | Some non-PI prompt surfaces can still receive OpenClaw workflow hints when tools are nonempty. | Not widened here. That belongs to the OpenClaw assembled-prompt surface, not the native Codex app-server lane. This PR keeps only Codex-specific subagent disambiguation in the Codex lane. | ##Adversarial Audit Addressed v2 After the first green run, a fresh read-only audit found two real merge-blocking precision issues and one proof-language overclaim: | Finding | Fix in this PR | | --- | --- | | `personalityMode: "codex"` kept Codex's native personality unset, but the bridge still told the model to use `SOUL.md` for personality. | The SOUL personality pointer now depends on the resolved app-server personality override, not raw SOUL presence. In `"codex"` mode, `SOUL.md` stays turn-input reference context and the bridge does not point personality at it. | | Matching `thread_bootstrap` context-engine resumes skipped old bootstrap history, but also dropped the current turn's `systemPromptAddition`. | Matching bootstrap resumes now omit the old bootstrap transcript while still projecting the current turn's `systemPromptAddition` as quoted reference context, not developer policy. | | The PR proof phrasing implied live proof of Codex actually loading `AGENTS.md`. | The body now says exactly what this PR proves: OpenClaw no longer sends `project_doc_max_bytes: 0`, so it no longer suppresses Codex's native project-doc loader. |

target.title: feat(approvals): add plain-language plugin approvals
target.body:
## Mission

Plugin approvals are a security boundary, but they are often delivered in chat where the old fallback text read like internal debug output. A normal approval could show a raw title, full command text, proposed exec policy, session key, tool/plugin IDs, expiry, and `/approve <id>` syntax all at once.

That raw format is useful for maintainers, but it is not easy for a Telegram/Matrix user to scan quickly. The goal of this PR is to make opt-in plugin approvals understandable at a glance while preserving the legacy raw behavior by default.

In short: keep the original technical approval prompt as the default, and add a plain-language approval mode for users who explicitly choose it.

## What This Adds

This PR adds `approvals.plugin.language` with three modes:

- `original`: existing raw technical plugin approval text. This remains the default.
- `simple`: a short plain-language prompt with `Action`, `It will`, `Risk`, and available choices.
- `simple-technical`: the plain-language prompt plus the raw technical fields below it.

Example config:

```json
{
  "approvals": {
    "plugin": {
      "language": "simple"
    }
  }
}
```

The new renderer keeps interactive approval metadata intact so native Telegram/Matrix approval buttons can still be rendered. Text-only channels still get an actionable `/approve plugin:<id> allow-once|allow-always|deny` fallback.

## Implementation

- Added the typed `PluginApprovalLanguage` config surface and validation.
- Wired the configured language through shared plugin approval rendering.
- Forwarded the language option through fallback delivery and native Telegram/Matrix approval payloads.
- Added plain-English approval output with risk text and decision-aware fallback instructions.
- Added `Command preview` output for commands that are high risk or cannot be summarized completely.
- Redacted token/password/API-key/bearer/basic-auth/cookie/userinfo forms from previews before they can be posted into chat.
- Updated generated config and plugin SDK API baselines.
- Added focused renderer and forwarder tests.

## Safety Model

The important rule is: if `simple` mode hides raw technical details, the summary must either explain the meaningful action or fail closed.

Fail closed means the approval becomes higher risk and shows a redacted command preview instead of pretending the command is harmless.

The renderer now handles and tests common Codex app-server command shapes:

- shell wrappers: `bash -lc`, `sh -c`, `zsh -c`, `env`, `timeout`, `set -a`, `source`;
- shell control: `&&`, `||`, `;`, background `&`, conditionals, loops, and attached redirections like `cat notes.txt>out.txt`;
- pipelines: known stages are summarized stage-by-stage; unknown stages become high risk with a preview;
- file reads/writes/deletes: `cat`, `sed`, `find`, `rm`, `mkdir`, `touch`, `cp`, `mv`, redirection writes;
- network transfers: `curl` and `wget` URL targets, upload operands, output operands, stdin upload via redirection, config/header/cookie/auth/netrc forms;
- credentials: bearer headers, OAuth bearer flags, basic auth, proxy auth, cookie headers, netrc credential sources, URL userinfo;
- dev/ops commands: `git`, `docker`, service managers, package managers, interpreters, logs/process inspection;
- remote commands: `ssh`, `scp`, SSH remote commands, and SSH local-execution options such as `ProxyCommand` and `LocalCommand`.

## Review And Hardening Trail

This PR went through a long approval-safety review loop because turning verbose technical prompts into plain language makes the summarizer security-sensitive.

Issues fixed during review:

- Preserved actionable text fallback instructions for channels without buttons.
- Made pipelines visible before raw command text can be hidden.
- Made command target extraction command-aware so boolean flags like `cat -n .env` do not hide sensitive targets.
- Surfaced curl/wget upload and output operands, including `.env` style local-file transfers.
- Handled stdin uploads such as `curl --data-binary @- ... < .env`.
- Redacted basic-auth, OAuth bearer, cookie, header, and URL credential forms from command previews.
- Failed closed on curl config files and curl header-file inputs.
- Surfaced wget credential/header/cookie options.
- Showed targets for redirected formatter writes like `printf ... > .env`.
- Handled background shell separators such as `sleep 1 & rm -rf /tmp/x`.
- Parsed Git global options such as `git -C repo reset --hard` before classifying subcommands.
- Treated `find` output-file predicates like `-fprint`, `-fprint0`, `-fls`, and `-fprintf` as writes.
- Detected attached shell redirections like `cat notes.txt>out.txt`.
- Failed closed on SSH local-execution options such as `ssh -o ProxyCommand=...` and `ssh -o LocalCommand=...`.
- Refreshed against latest `upstream/main` repeatedly and regenerated the config baseline after conflicts.
- Stabilized cron test mock state exposed by the refreshed CI shard.

The latest ClawSweeper review no longer reports a concrete repair item. It now asks for human maintainer review of the opt-in approval-safety boundary and branch scope.

## Current Status

- Latest head at time of this update: `91d6303a0e`.
- GitHub reports the PR mergeable and clean.
- CI/checks are green on the latest head.
- `Real behavior proof` is successful.
- ClawSweeper durable review status: needs maintainer review before merge, not an automated repair.
- Remaining decision: maintainers need to accept the opt-in plain-language approval UX/security boundary, or ask for scope splitting if they want the unrelated refreshed-base/test-stability churn separated.

## Verification

Local verification after the latest SSH hardening and main refresh:

- `pnpm config:docs:check`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.unit-fast.config.ts src/plugin-sdk/approval-renderers.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.infra.config.ts src/infra/plugin-approval-forwarder.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p tsconfig.core.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/core.tsbuildinfo`
- `pnpm check:test-types`
- `pnpm lint --threads=8`

Earlier focused verification also covered config schema/help quality, plugin SDK API baseline, gateway/channel state, and live renderer output.

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: Chat-delivered plugin approvals can now render as a short plain-English approval card instead of a raw technical dump when `approvals.plugin.language` is set to `simple`. The old raw format remains the default through `original`.
- Real environment tested: Hadi's live OpenClaw source checkout on Ubuntu 24.04 (`/home/hadi/src/openclaw`), the PR worktree, and Telegram channel status in polling mode.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:
  - Rendered real approval messages through `buildPluginApprovalRequestMessage(..., { language: "simple" })`.
  - Ran focused approval renderer and forwarder tests.
  - Ran type, lint, config docs, and baseline checks.
  - Cherry-picked the feature into the live source checkout during testing, ran `pnpm build`, restarted `openclaw-gateway.service`, and checked gateway/Telegram channel state.
- Evidence after fix: terminal renderer output showed the intended card shape:

```text
🛡️ Approval needed
Action
Create/check workspace files or folders

It will
- create folder(s): memory
- check whether a condition or file exists: memory/context.md

Risk: Low
Only creates or checks workspace files. No network, service, or delete step detected.

Choose below.
If buttons are unavailable, reply: /approve plugin:example allow-once|allow-always|deny
```

Pipeline and unknown-stage proof:

```text
--- pipeline curl ---
🛡️ Approval needed
Action
Use the network or download data

It will
- read file contents: notes.txt
- make a network request or download data: https://example.test/upload

Risk: Medium
Network commands can send or fetch data outside this machine.

Choose below.
If buttons are unavailable, reply: /approve plugin:pipeline allow-once|allow-always|deny

--- unknown pipeline ---
🛡️ Approval needed
Action
Run a terminal command

It will
- read file contents: notes.txt
- run custom-uploader

Command preview
cat notes.txt | custom-uploader --send

Risk: High
This command is part of a pipeline I cannot fully summarize, so review it before approving.

Choose below.
If buttons are unavailable, reply: /approve plugin:unknown allow-once|allow-always|deny
```

Live channel state checked during proof:

```text
Telegram default (Telegram): enabled, configured, running, connected, mode:polling, token:tokenFile
```

Observed result after fix:

- `simple` mode no longer includes the raw technical details block.
- Escaped shell separators such as `&&` are decoded before parsing.
- Shell conditionals no longer surface as fake `run if` / `run then` actions.
- Non-shell pipeline stages are visible or fail closed with a high-risk preview.
- Sensitive command previews are redacted before being shown in chat.
- SSH local-execution options now fail closed with a high-risk preview.

- What was not tested:

- The Mantis Telegram Desktop GIF run skipped instead of producing before/after GIFs. This PR still has terminal renderer proof, live gateway/channel state proof, earlier Telegram screenshot proof, and a successful `Real behavior proof` check.

target.title: fix(tools): sanitize LLM sentinel tokens in bash exec and web-fetch URL inputs
target.body:
## Summary

Related patches that defend OpenClaw against LLM sentinel-token leakage in tool-call arguments. Smaller models (Gemma 4 26B in our deployment) occasionally emit `<|...|>`, `<|<|"`, `<<|"|`, and similar special tokens at the head of `bash.exec` `command`, `web-fetch` `url`, or file-tool `path`/`file_path` arguments. Without sanitization these flow into `bash -c '<|<|"grep ...'`, `fetch('<|"https://...')`, or `read({file_path: '<<|"|https://...'})`, producing syntax/parse/`ENOENT` errors and forcing the model to hallucinate an answer.

All patches:
- **No-op for clean input** — zero impact on Claude (Opus/Sonnet), Mistral, Llama, or any model that does not leak tokens.
- **Logged** — when sanitize changes the input, an event (`bash.sanitize_special_tokens` / `webfetch.sanitize_special_tokens` / `tool.sanitize_special_tokens`) is emitted for monitoring.

## Commits

| commit | scope | files |
|--------|-------|-------|
| `7cbc14d38` | web-fetch URL sanitize | sanitize-url.ts/test, web-fetch.ts, fetch-guard.ts |
| `fe897846b` | bash exec command sanitize | sanitize-command.ts/test (14 cases), bash-tools.exec.ts |
| `d1265be6b` | `<<` sentinel variants + file-tool path args | sanitize-command.ts/test (22 cases), sanitize-url.ts, sanitize-tool-args.ts/test (9 cases), pi-tools.params.ts |

The bash patch mirrors the URL patch's structure exactly (same predicate set, same trim semantics, same log shape). The follow-up commit `d1265be6b` reuses the same sentinel-stripping library for file-tool path args via the single `normalizeToolParams` chokepoint (read/write/edit and the workspace-root / memory-flush wrappers all route through it), and content-bearing args (`content`/`oldText`/`newText`) are intentionally left untouched so file writes are never mutated.

## Compatibility

Sanitize functions are pure and side-effect-free outside the log emission. Untouched on clean input; safe to land before any regression test runs against the broader exec/fetch/file pipelines. False-positive guards keep a lone leading `<x>` (HTML-like text), a lone leading `"` quote, and bash heredocs (`cat <<EOF`) intact — only token-like leading runs (`>= 2` chars containing `<`) and the `<<|...|` mid-command form are stripped.

## Co-authored

Drafted with Claude Opus 4.7 assistance.

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed**: Smaller models — specifically Gemma 4 26B NVFP4 in our deployment — leak LLM sentinel tokens (`<|...|>`, `<|<|"`, `<|"`, `<<|"|`, etc.) at the head of `bash.exec` `command` arguments, `web-fetch` `url` arguments, and file-tool `path`/`file_path` arguments. Without sanitization, the shell receives a command like `<|"|grep something` and returns `bash: syntax error near unexpected token '<|'`. The agent then has no useful tool output and proceeds to fabricate an answer — hallucination caused by silent tool-side failure rather than by model-side reasoning error. The identical failure mode appears on the URL side: `fetch('<|"https://example.com/...')` parses as an invalid URL; and on the file-tool side: `read({file_path: '<<|"|https://...'})` resolves to `ENOENT .../workspace/<|<|` and the agent fabricates file content.

**Real environment tested**: spark-home (DGX Spark GB10, ARM64 Ubuntu 24, 128 GB unified memory). OpenClaw runs under PM2 as a long-lived process via a wrapper script (no `pm2 restart`; reload is `pm2 stop` + `pm2 start` of the wrapper to preserve env). The model backend is Gemma 4 26B NVFP4 served by a local vLLM container on `http://127.0.0.1:8005`. Real Telegram-channel user sessions, not unit fixtures, with the agent named `gemma` (`@lisyoen_gemma_bot`).

**Exact steps or command run after this patch**:

1. Built OpenClaw locally on the branch containing `7cbc14d38`, `fe897846b`, and the follow-up `d1265be6b`.
2. Reloaded OpenClaw via the wrapper (`pm2 stop openclaw && pm2 start openclaw`, wrapper as `pm_exec_path`) to load the patched dist. New gateway PID, port 9086 LISTEN, stable uptime confirmed.
3. Issued real user prompts to the Gemma agent over Telegram that previously caused hallucinated answers — the same prompts that earlier produced `<|"|grep ...` as the bash `command` and `<<|"|https://...` as a `read` `file_path`.
4. Tailed live console output from the running openclaw process: `pm2 logs openclaw --lines 200 --nostream | grep -E 'sanitize_special_tokens'`
5. Compared the new agent answers against the on-disk files to verify content match (no fabrication).

**Evidence after fix**: Redacted runtime logs captured from the live `openclaw` PM2 process on spark-home (KST = UTC+9, command bodies redacted to length-only because they contain user data; the trimmed delta is what the patch removes):

```
2026-05-17 08:13:19  bash.sanitize_special_tokens  original_len=6    sanitized_len=1
2026-05-17 08:13:20  bash.sanitize_special_tokens  original_len=61   sanitized_len=56
2026-05-17 08:13:22  bash.sanitize_special_tokens  original_len=61   sanitized_len=56
2026-05-17 08:13:23  bash.sanitize_special_tokens  original_len=42   sanitized_len=37
2026-05-17 08:13:35  bash.sanitize_special_tokens  original_len=42   sanitized_len=37
2026-05-17 08:21:33  bash.sanitize_special_tokens  original_len=121  sanitized_len=116
2026-05-17 08:21:35  bash.sanitize_special_tokens  original_len=73   sanitized_len=68
2026-05-17 08:45:45  bash.sanitize_special_tokens  original_len=73   sanitized_len=68
```

Each line above is a real Gemma 4 26B tool-call. The trimmed delta is consistently around 5 bytes, matching the design (leading `<|...|>` prefix strip with no false positives on the remainder of the command). Forensic artifacts retained on the deployment and available on request (contain user data so not attached here): the quarantined pre-fix session jsonl at `~/.openclaw/agents/gemma/sessions/.quarantine-fresh-20260517-081701/cb03f312-6f71-4a36-a8c2-a16736300294.jsonl` and the pre-fix `sessions.json` backup containing the leaking `agent:gemma:*` mappings.

**Observed result after fix**: The shell no longer receives `<|...|>` prefixed commands. Every `bash.sanitize_special_tokens` event in the runtime log above corresponds to a leak that was successfully trimmed before reaching `bash -c`; the resulting commands execute cleanly. The same Telegram prompts that produced fabricated answers approximately 24 hours before the fix now return the actual on-disk file contents quoted verbatim. Side-by-side comparison confirmed accuracy: every key fact from the pre-fix fabricated answer was invented; every key fact from the post-fix answer matches the on-disk card. Clean commands (no sentinel prefix, the dominant case under Opus/Sonnet sessions) pass through untouched and emit no event, confirming the no-op-for-clean-input design.

**Additional pattern observed 2026-05-17 11:13 KST (follow-up commit `d1265be6b`)**: A smaller-model leak of the form `<<|"|https://...` (double-`<` prefix, no closing `|>`) bypassed the original `<\|[^|>]*\|>` regex, and the leak landed in a `read` tool `file_path` argument — a path not previously sanitized (only `bash.exec` and `web-fetch` URL were). The quarantined session shows `read({file_path: "<<|\"|https://..."})` resolving to `ENOENT, access '.../workspace/<|<|'`, ten consecutive failed toolResults, and the model then emitting a fabricated "success" reply. The follow-up commit extends the regex to strip token-like leading `<`+ runs (`<<|"|`, `<<|`, `<<`, `<<<`) and the mid-command `<<|...|` form, and applies the same sentinel-stripping to file-tool `path`/`file_path` args through the shared `normalizeToolParams` chokepoint (read/write/edit). Unit coverage grew from 14 to 22 sanitize-command cases plus 9 new sanitize-tool-args cases (38 sanitize tests total, all green), including explicit false-positive guards (`<x>` and a lone leading `"` are preserved). After reload, the new `tool.sanitize_special_tokens` event fires for the file-tool path leaks and the `read` call resolves the real path. Forensic jsonl preserved at `~/.openclaw/agents/gemma/sessions/.quarantine-hallucination-20260517-1113/663a8752-71d0-4826-aaed-a9777f741757.jsonl`.

**What was not tested**: Behavior under non-Gemma frontier models that emit similar prefixes — Opus and Sonnet in our deployment did not exhibit this leakage at the moment of testing, so we could not produce a positive sanitize event for them; the patches are no-op for them by design and that no-op behavior is exercised by the included unit tests. Sanitize event rates under heavy concurrent multi-agent load were also not exercised on spark-home where typical concurrency is at most 3. A live Telegram round-trip on the exact 11:13 prompt after the follow-up reload is pending the operator's own Telegram client (the gateway-side path sanitize and unit suite are verified).

target.title: fix(agents): strip malformed `required` from tool schemas
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: Some schema generators (e.g. TypeBox) emit `required: null` instead of omitting the field. Providers like Bailian GLM-5 reject this outright with `at '/required': got null, want array`.
- Why it matters: Tool calling is completely broken for any provider that validates `required` must be an array (OpenAPI spec mandates this).
- What changed: `normalizeToolParameters()` now strips non-array `required` values early, before any provider-specific cleaning runs. The Gemini schema-cleaning loop also guards against non-array `required`.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): Valid `required` arrays pass through unchanged. No changes to union flattening, provider detection, or any other schema normalization logic.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Skills / tool execution

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #40568

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Tool calling with providers that enforce strict JSON Schema validation (e.g. Bailian GLM-5) now works correctly when tool schemas contain `required: null`.

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? No
- New/changed network calls? No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? No
- Data access scope changed? No

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Any
- Runtime/container: Node 22+
- Model/provider: Bailian GLM-5 (or any provider with strict `required` validation)
- Integration/channel (if any): N/A
- Relevant config (redacted): Tool with `required: null` in its JSON Schema

### Steps

1. Configure a tool whose schema generator emits `required: null` (e.g. TypeBox with an optional-only object)
2. Send a tool-calling request to Bailian GLM-5
3. Observe the error: `at '/required': got null, want array`

### Expected

- Tool call succeeds; `required: null` is silently stripped before sending to the provider

### Actual (before fix)

- Provider rejects the schema with `got null, want array`

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after

| Test | What it verifies |
|------|-----------------|
| `strips top-level required: null` | Core fix: `required: null` is removed from schema |
| `preserves valid required array` | No regression: valid `["foo"]` arrays survive |
| `strips required: undefined-like values (non-array)` | Edge case: string `required` also stripped |
| `handles required: null with Gemini provider` | Gemini path also handles the fix |
| `returns tool unchanged when parameters is absent` | No-op path is unaffected |

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios: All 5 unit tests pass; `required: null`, valid array, string value, Gemini provider, absent parameters
- Edge cases checked: `required: "bar"` (non-array non-null), missing `parameters` entirely
- What you did **not** verify: Live Bailian GLM-5 API call (no access to the provider)

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? Yes
- Config/env changes? No
- Migration needed? No

## Failure Recovery (if this breaks)

- How to disable/revert this change quickly: Revert the single commit
- Files/config to restore: `src/agents/pi-tools.schema.ts`, `src/agents/schema/clean-for-gemini.ts`
- Known bad symptoms reviewers should watch for: Tool schemas losing their `required` field when it should be present (would show as all parameters becoming optional)

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: Recursive call in `normalizeToolParameters` for `required: null` schemas
  - Mitigation: Recursion depth is always 1 — the stripped schema no longer has `required` in it, so the guard condition is not hit again

target.title: feat(discord): archive ACP-created threads on /acp close
target.body:
## Summary
When `/acp close` is used inside a Discord thread that OpenClaw itself created for an ACP session, OpenClaw now:
1. closes the ACP session
2. unbinds the thread/session
3. archives the Discord thread

This reduces stale task-thread clutter while preserving history.

## Scope
In scope:
- Discord threads only
- ACP-created threads only
- archive, not delete

Out of scope:
- Telegram topics / Slack reply threads / generic cross-channel conversation closing
- deleting threads
- archiving manually attached or reused existing threads
- idle-time auto-archive

## Behavior
- ACP-created Discord thread: `/acp close` closes, unbinds, then archives the thread
- manually attached/reused thread: existing close behavior remains unchanged
- archive failure: close/unbind still succeed and the reply is downgraded to a partial-success warning
- missing ownership metadata fails safe (no archive)

## Implementation
- Thread binding metadata now records whether the thread was spawned by ACP
- `/acp close` checks that metadata before requesting Discord thread archive
- Discord outbound payload handling supports archive-after-reply so the success message is sent before the thread is archived

## Tests
Added/updated tests for:
- ACP-created Discord thread bindings storing spawned metadata
- `/acp close` archiving ACP-created Discord threads
- `/acp close` not archiving manually attached Discord threads
- fail-safe behavior when ownership metadata is incomplete
- non-Discord behavior unchanged
- Discord outbound archive-after-reply success and partial-failure behavior

Executed:
- `npx vitest run src/auto-reply/reply/commands-acp.test.ts src/channels/plugins/outbound/discord.test.ts src/discord/monitor/thread-bindings.lifecycle.test.ts`

target.title: Add configurable outputDir for session-memory hook
target.body:
## Summary

Describe the problem and fix in 2–5 bullets:

- Problem: `session-memory` always writes `/new` and `/reset` snapshots into `<workspace>/memory/`, which mixes session continuity artifacts into active semantic memory.
- Why it matters: agents using `memory_search` or local embeddings end up indexing session dumps alongside long-term notes, which adds noise and indexing cost.
- What changed: added an optional `hooks.internal.entries.session-memory.outputDir` config, kept the default as `memory`, validated it as workspace-relative, and documented the new behavior.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): default output location, slug generation, transcript extraction, and the hook's enabled/disabled semantics.

target.title: config: use datetime suffix for config backup files instead of numeric rotation
target.body:
Closes #39923

## Summary

Replaces the numeric rotation ring (`openclaw.json.bak.1`–`.bak.4`) with datetime-stamped backup files (`openclaw.json.bak.YYYYMMDD-HHmmssSSS`, UTC). This makes it immediately clear *when* each backup was taken without needing to inspect file metadata — directly useful when recovering from a bad config change.

**Before:**
```
openclaw.json.bak
openclaw.json.bak.1   ← newest rotation slot
openclaw.json.bak.2
openclaw.json.bak.3
openclaw.json.bak.4   ← oldest rotation slot
```

**After:**
```
openclaw.json.bak
openclaw.json.bak.20260308-143022456   ← most recent
openclaw.json.bak.20260308-130015000
openclaw.json.bak.20260308-110000000
openclaw.json.bak.20260308-090000000   ← oldest kept
```

## Changes

- `formatBackupTimestamp(date)` — new helper producing `YYYYMMDD-HHmmssSSS` (UTC, millisecond precision to avoid collision on rapid saves; lexicographically sortable without reading file metadata)
- `BACKUP_DATETIME_RE` — exported regex for matching valid datetime suffixes
- `rotateConfigBackups` — now renames `.bak` → `.bak.<timestamp>` instead of chaining renames across numbered slots; accepts optional `now` param for deterministic testing
- `hardenBackupPermissions` — scans directory for datetime-pattern `.bak.*` entries and chmods them (in addition to the primary `.bak`)
- `cleanOrphanBackups` — replaces the `validSuffixes` integer set with datetime-pattern matching; deletes all non-datetime `.bak.*` files (including legacy `.bak.1`–`.bak.4`); keeps only the `CONFIG_BACKUP_COUNT - 1` most recent datetime backups via lexicographic sort
- `maintainConfigBackups` — passes `now` through to `rotateConfigBackups`; `readdir` promoted to required on `BackupMaintenanceFs` (production `fs.promises` always has it)

## Test plan

- `formatBackupTimestamp` — UTC formatting, padding, lexicographic sort order, regex match
- Ring test — 6 writes with fixed timestamps confirms 4 datetime backups kept, oldest pruned, `.bak` holds most-recent-minus-one
- `hardenBackupPermissions` — verifies 0o600 on both `.bak` and `.bak.<datetime>` (POSIX only)
- `cleanOrphanBackups` — legacy numeric/PID/manual orphans deleted; datetime files preserved; limit enforcement with `CONFIG_BACKUP_COUNT + 1` overflow
- `maintainConfigBackups` — full compose flow: prior `.bak` gets a datetime stamp, new `.bak` created, permissions hardened, orphans pruned

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

target.title: feat: inject sessionId into runtime context
target.body:
## Summary

Expose the current session UUID in the `Runtime:` line of the system prompt, so agents can reference it in memory files and cross-session links without needing to infer it from filesystem paths.

## Motivation

Currently, the agent has no way to know its own session ID from the runtime context. The only workaround is reading the most recent `.jsonl` filename from disk — fragile and unnecessary when the value is already available in `sessionEntry`.

Use case: when an agent logs events to daily memory files, it helps to include `session: <uuid>` references for traceability. This is impossible without the session ID in context.

## Changes

3 files, +5 lines:

- **`system-prompt-params.ts`** — add `sessionId?: string` to `RuntimeInfoInput`
- **`system-prompt.ts`** — add `sessionId` to the `buildRuntimeLine` type signatures and render `session=<uuid>` in the Runtime line
- **`commands-system-prompt.ts`** — pass `params.sessionEntry?.sessionId` into the runtime object

The Runtime line will now include:
```
Runtime: agent=main | session=04163e3f-... | host=... | ...
```

## Testing

- `tsc --noEmit` passes
- All 50 tests in `system-prompt.test.ts` pass
- No breaking changes (field is optional)

target.title: status: surface global tool profile hints in overview
target.body:
## Summary
- surface the configured global tool profile baseline in `openclaw status` overview output
- surface the configured global tool profile baseline in `openclaw status --all` overview output
- add a practical hint when the global `tools.profile` is set to `messaging`

## Why
A messaging-first setup can feel unexpectedly limited even when the configuration is valid. Surfacing the global tool profile baseline directly in status output makes that easier to diagnose without digging through config files, while staying explicit that per-agent and provider overrides may differ.

## Changes
- add a `Global tools profile` overview row to `openclaw status`
- add the same `Global tools profile` overview row to `openclaw status --all`
- include a practical hint for `tools.profile: "messaging"`
- include a short descriptive note for `tools.profile: "full"`
- keep the wording scoped to the global config baseline rather than the effective resolved runtime profile

## What did not change
- no runtime behavior
- no tool policy logic
- no config schema values
- no effective-profile resolution logic

## Change Type
- [ ] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor
- [x] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope
- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR
- Related #39954
- Related #39963

## User-visible / Behavior Changes
`openclaw status` and `openclaw status --all` now show the configured global tool profile baseline in the Overview section when one is set. For `messaging`, the output explains that it is intentionally narrow by design and points users toward `tools.profile: "full"` for a broader global command/control baseline. Per-agent and provider overrides may still differ.

## Security Impact
- New permissions/capabilities? (`No`)
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`No`)
- New/changed network calls? (`No`)
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`No`)
- Data access scope changed? (`No`)
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation:

## Repro + Verification
### Environment
- OS: Windows 11
- Runtime/container: local clone
- Model/provider: n/a
- Integration/channel (if any): n/a
- Relevant config (redacted): `tools.profile`

### Steps
1. Set `tools.profile` in config (for example `messaging` or `full`)
2. Run `openclaw status`
3. Run `openclaw status --all`
4. Inspect the Overview section

### Expected
- status output surfaces the configured global tool profile baseline when one is set
- `messaging` output includes a practical hint that the global profile is intentionally narrow

### Actual
- updated status overview code now adds a `Global tools profile` row in both commands

## Evidence
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification
- Verified scenarios: reviewed the exact diff and confirmed the row is added to both status renderers; user verified the behavior in a local dev environment.
- Edge cases checked: no row is shown when no tool profile is set; other profiles still render plainly; wording stays scoped to the global config baseline rather than implying effective runtime resolution.
- What I did not personally verify: cross-environment behavior outside the user's local dev setup.

## Review Conversations
- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration
- Backward compatible? (`Yes`)

- How to disable/revert this change quickly: revert this commit
- Files/config to restore: `src/commands/status.command.ts`, `src/commands/status-all.ts`, `src/commands/status.tool-profile.ts`
- Known bad symptoms reviewers should watch for: misleading wording or overly verbose status output

## Risks and Mitigations
- Risk: maintainers may prefer the hint in a different status surface or wording style

target.title: feat(agent): add text repetition guard for streaming output
target.body:
## Problem

LLMs (especially Gemini) can enter self-reinforcing text generation loops where they repeatedly emit the same patterns without invoking any tools. For example:

```
Wait, I should check item 1... Done. Sent.
Wait, I should check item 2... Done. Sent.
Wait, I should check item 3... Done. Sent.
... (repeats indefinitely until token limit)
```

The existing `tool-loop-detection` module only monitors tool calls. When a model loops in pure text output, there is currently **no detection or abort mechanism**, causing:
- Token/cost waste (entire context window consumed by repetition)
- User-facing message spam
- Session becoming unrecoverable

## Related Issues

- #25627 — Coder agent repeats messages on exec running retries (text repetition aspect)
- #24800 — Context overflow from undetected loops
- #40396 — Agent trapped in infinite thought loop after upgrade

## Solution

Add a `text-repetition-guard` module that detects streaming text-generation loops using four complementary strategies:

1. **N-gram frequency** — Detects repeated phrases via sliding window
2. **Consecutive identical lines** — Catches copy-paste style repetition
3. **Suffix cycle detection** — Identifies repeating tail patterns
4. **Line-group cycle detection** — Catches multi-line block repetition

The guard is integrated into `handleMessageUpdate` and runs every `checkIntervalChars` characters (default: 200). On detection, it sets an `abortedByTextRepetitionGuard` flag and calls `session.abort()`.

### Key implementation details

- **Config** integrated into standard `ToolsConfig` / `AgentToolsConfig` system
- **Resolved config cached** at message start (zero per-tick resolution overhead)
- **False-positive mitigations**: degenerate content filtering, progressive content detection (e.g. "Step 1..., Step 2..."), n-gram source-line diversity check
- **blockChunker drain prevention**: When the guard fires, a state flag prevents `handleMessageEnd` from draining buffered repetitive content (addresses Greptile review feedback)

## Tests

15 test cases covering all 4 detection strategies, edge cases, progressive content (no false positive), and configuration.

target.title: fix(googlechat): clean up typing message on NO_REPLY
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes #39843

When a Google Chat agent reacts with an emoji via `reactions.create` and then returns `NO_REPLY`, the "_Clawdbot is typing..._" message persists indefinitely because the `deliver` callback is never invoked.

**Root cause:** The Google Chat channel sends a typing indicator as a real message (`_BotName is typing..._`), stored in `typingMessageName`. The `deliver` callback clears this by either updating or deleting it. When the agent returns NO_REPLY, `deliver` is never called and the typing message is left orphaned.

**Fix:** After `dispatchReplyWithBufferedBlockDispatcher` returns, check if `typingMessageName` is still set (meaning `deliver` was never called). If so, delete the typing message. This mirrors the pattern used by Discord (#27039) and other channels for NO_REPLY cleanup.

## Test plan

- [x] TypeScript check passes
- [x] Verified `deleteGoogleChatMessage` is already imported and used in the same file
- [x] The cleanup only fires when `typingMessageName` is still set (deliver was never called)
- [x] Error during cleanup is caught and logged, not thrown

target.title: Emit model.usage diagnostic event from CLI agent path
target.body:
## Summary

- The `model.usage` diagnostic event was only emitted from the reply-runner path (Telegram/WhatsApp/heartbeat), meaning CLI agent runs via `openclaw agent` were invisible to OTEL-based token tracking
- This adds the same emission pattern to `agentCommandInternal()` in `src/commands/agent.ts`, after `updateSessionStoreAfterAgentRun()` completes
- Uses the identical structure from `agent-runner.ts`: extracts usage from `result.meta.agentMeta`, computes cost via `resolveModelCostConfig`/`estimateUsageCost`, and calls `emitDiagnosticEvent` with full token breakdown

## Motivation

When using the diagnostics-otel plugin to track token usage across OpenClaw instances, CLI agent sessions (`openclaw agent`) produced zero OTEL metrics. Only sessions originating from messaging channels (Telegram, WhatsApp, heartbeat) emitted `model.usage` events. This made it impossible to reconcile OTEL-reported usage against cloud provider billing (Bedrock/Vertex) for CLI-heavy deployments.

## Test plan

- [ ] Verify TypeScript compilation passes (confirmed: `tsc --noEmit` reports no new errors)
- [ ] Linter passes (confirmed: biome reports 0 warnings, 0 errors)
- [ ] Run `openclaw agent` with diagnostics-otel enabled and verify `model.usage` events appear in the OTEL collector output
- [ ] Verify reply-runner path still emits as before (no regression)
- [ ] Compare OTEL token totals against cloud billing to confirm coverage improvement

target.title: fix: cap effective session context window by known model limit
target.body:
Fixes #39857

## Summary

- Problem: `resolveContextTokensForModel()` could return an override value even when OpenClaw already knew the selected model's smaller context window.
- Why it matters: that inflated value could be persisted into session metadata and surfaced in `/status`, so OpenClaw would over-report the effective context window for the active model.
- What changed: when both values are known, the effective window is now `min(override, modelWindow)`; provider aliases are also normalized before reading configured model windows.
- What did NOT change: if the model window is unknown, override behavior stays the same; this does not change provider-side request limits or widen any model window.

## Problem

This was a correctness bug in the effective context-window resolver, not just a display nit.

Before this PR, the resolver could treat `agents.defaults.contextTokens` (or another context override) as authoritative even when the selected model's real window was already known and smaller.

That meant OpenClaw could persist and report a context window that the active model does not actually have.

Affected paths include places that read the resolved `contextTokens`, such as:

- session store metadata
- `/status`
- other callers that rely on persisted/effective session context

## Example

If a user sets:

- `agents.defaults.contextTokens: 1048000`

and then uses a model whose known context window is smaller, OpenClaw could previously keep reporting `1048000` instead of the model's real effective limit.

The correct result is the smaller effective window.

## Fix

The functional change is intentionally narrow:

- if an override exists and the selected model window is also known, return the smaller value
- if the model window is unknown, preserve the previous override behavior
- when reading configured model windows from `cfg.models.providers`, normalize provider names first so aliases like `bedrock` still match configured entries like `amazon-bedrock`

## Why this is safe

This is fail-safe and correctness-preserving:

- it only removes overstatement
- it never increases an effective context window
- it does not change behavior when OpenClaw does not know the model's real window

## Validation

- `bunx vitest run src/agents/context.test.ts src/agents/models-config.merge.test.ts`
- `bunx vitest run src/plugins/loader.test.ts`
- `pnpm check`

## Scope note

A few later commits in this PR are CI-only follow-ups caused by `main` moving during review. The product fix itself remains limited to effective context-window resolution and regression coverage for that bug.

target.title: discord: include accountId in no-mention auto-reply logs
target.body:
## What
Include `accountId` (and `guildId`) in the `discord-auto-reply` log line emitted when a guild message is dropped due to mention gating (`reason: "no-mention"`).

## Why
When multiple Discord accounts/providers are configured (e.g. `default` + another bot account), the current log message is ambiguous:

```json
{ "channelId": "...", "reason": "no-mention" }
```

This can look like a config bug (e.g. `requireMention: false` seemingly not taking effect), while in reality it may be a different account/provider instance performing the drop.

Adding `accountId` makes field diagnosis significantly easier.

## Tests
- `vitest run src/discord/monitor/message-handler.preflight.test.ts`

target.title: fix: echo xAI OAuth PKCE challenge on token exchange
target.body:
## Summary

- Echo `code_challenge` and `code_challenge_method=S256` in the xAI authorization-code token POST, alongside the existing `code_verifier`.
- Add focused regression coverage for the xAI token request body.
- Leave the xAI refresh-token exchange unchanged.

## Why

xAI can reject the authorization-code token exchange with `code_challenge is required` unless the original PKCE challenge is included at the token endpoint. Standard PKCE only requires `code_verifier`, but sending the challenge fields is accepted by OAuth token endpoints and fixes this xAI compatibility failure.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: xAI OAuth authorization-code token exchange now sends the PKCE verifier plus the echoed PKCE challenge and method expected by xAI.
- **Real environment tested**: Local OpenClaw PR worktree on macOS, branch `codex/xai-oauth-pkce-token-fields`, after `pnpm build` produced `dist/extensions/xai/xai-oauth.js`.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:

```bash
pnpm build
node -e "import('./dist/extensions/xai/xai-oauth.js').then(({ buildXaiOAuthAuthorizationCodeTokenBody }) => { const body = buildXaiOAuthAuthorizationCodeTokenBody({ code: 'AUTHCODE-redacted', codeVerifier: 'VERIFIER-redacted', codeChallenge: 'CHALLENGE-redacted' }); console.log(JSON.stringify(body, null, 2)); })"
```

- **Evidence after fix**:

```json
{
  "grant_type": "authorization_code",
  "code": "AUTHCODE-redacted",
  "redirect_uri": "http://127.0.0.1:56121/callback",
  "client_id": "b1a00492-073a-47ea-816f-4c329264a828",
  "code_verifier": "VERIFIER-redacted",
  "code_challenge": "CHALLENGE-redacted",
  "code_challenge_method": "S256"
}
```

- **Observed result after fix**: The built OpenClaw xAI OAuth module returns an authorization-code token body that includes `code_verifier`, `code_challenge`, and `code_challenge_method=S256`.
- **What was not tested**: A live browser login against a real xAI account/token redemption was not completed locally.

## Testing

- `pnpm exec vitest run --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-providers.config.ts --reporter=dot`
  - `172 passed | 1 skipped` test files
  - `1555 passed | 1 skipped` tests
- `pnpm build`

target.title: feat(anthropic): claude-cli-interactive backend — stream reasoning via local TLS proxy (experimental)
target.body:
## Summary

- **Problem.** OpenClaw's `claude-cli` backend invokes `claude -p --output-format stream-json` (headless). Starting **June 15, 2026** that path moves onto Anthropic's separate programmatic credit pool ([VentureBeat coverage](https://venturebeat.com/technology/anthropic-reinstates-openclaw-and-third-party-agent-usage-on-claude-subscriptions-with-a-catch)) — fixed monthly allowance, billed at API rates, doesn't roll over.
- **Why it matters.** Every OpenClaw Opus turn on a Claude subscription will drain that fixed credit post–June 15 instead of counting against the interactive subscription limits subscribers are paying for. Opus turns exhaust the credit fast.
- **What changed.** New opt-in `claude-cli-interactive` backend. Each turn spawns one fresh `bun wrapper.ts …` that boots a loopback HTTPS MITM on a 127.0.0.1 port, taps every API SSE event, re-emits the events as `claude -p --output-format stream-json` JSONL records, and kills the claude child on `end_turn`. A `tty-spoof.cjs` `NODE_OPTIONS=--require` preload keeps claude in interactive mode under piped stdio.
- **What did NOT change (scope boundary).** Existing `claude-cli` backend untouched. `agents.defaults.cliBackends.claude-cli` user overrides inherit into the interactive backend via `inheritUserConfigFrom` with `-p`-mode flags stripped. System CA store is not mutated (`NODE_EXTRA_CA_CERTS` scoped to the spawned claude child only). Non–`api.anthropic.com` CONNECT tunnels pass through unmodified. Tool-use rendering / waiting-on-tool injection lands in #82285, not here.
- **AI-assisted:** yes — drafted with Claude.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed:** `claude` running under OpenClaw in interactive (subscription) mode with full `init` / `stream_event*` / `result` JSONL reconstruction on the wrapper's stdout, sourced from the loopback MITM proxy's SSE tap.
- **Real environment tested:** Windows 11 Pro 26200, Node v24.12.0, pnpm, Bun, `@anthropic-ai/claude-code` v2.1.142 (npm-installed), Opus 4.7 (`claude-opus-4-7`), live OpenClaw gateway driving the backend per-turn over Telegram.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** `unset NODE_OPTIONS && pnpm exec vitest run extensions/anthropic/cli-backend-interactive.test.ts`; `pnpm exec tsc --noEmit -p extensions/anthropic/tsconfig.json`; set `cliBackends.claude-cli-interactive` active in user config; restart the OpenClaw gateway; send a Telegram message that triggers an Opus 4.7 turn; observe wrapper boot in stderr, SSE flow through `127.0.0.1:<port>`, JSONL arriving at OpenClaw; send a follow-up message with `--resume <sessionId>` to verify resume.
- **Evidence after fix:**
  - vitest: `Test Files 1 passed (1) / Tests 5 passed (5)` — `normalizeConfig` branches (inherited claude-cli match, direct Bun-path override, env passthrough, no-override skip).
  - Gateway log excerpt + cert listing + active-config snapshot on the [`proof-assets/pr-81851`](https://github.com/anagnorisis2peripeteia/openclaw/tree/proof-assets/pr-81851/.proof) branch. Embedded sample (one of three real turns in the gateway-log excerpt):

    ```
    cli exec: provider=claude-cli-interactive model=opus useResume=true session=present
    cli argv: bun <NPM>\…\wrapper.ts --effort max --verbose --setting-sources user --resume <SESSION> --allowedTools mcp__openclaw__* --model opus <prompt:N chars>
    [openclaw-proxy] certs ready: <HOME>\.openclaw\proxy-certs\ca.crt
    [openclaw-proxy] MITM proxy active on 127.0.0.1:17081 — Anthropic API traffic intercepted locally to capture thinking_delta events
    [openclaw-proxy] spawning claude binary: <NPM>\…\@anthropic-ai\claude-code\bin\claude.exe
    [openclaw-proxy] result flushed, waiting for session write before kill
    [openclaw-proxy] session file updated, killing claude
    ```
- **Observed result after fix:** per-turn lifecycle terminates cleanly — wrapper emits `init`, a continuous stream of `stream_event` records, then a single `result` on `end_turn`, sets `intentionalKill = true`, and `claude.kill()`s the child so the per-turn process tree exits 0.
- **What was not tested:** Linux/macOS (developed on Windows 11 only); bundled-MCP `claude-config-file` mode end-to-end with multiple MCP servers active (only `mcp__openclaw__*` exercised live); forced Anthropic-side `429` / `overloaded_error` mid-stream (the `api_error` synthetic event path is implemented but I did not provoke a real 429).

## Change Type

- [x] Feature

## Scope

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Integrations

## Linked

- Related: #81550 (programmatic-mode reasoning bridge — this PR is the interactive-mode equivalent on a separate backend); #82285 (tool-use injection, builds on the `stream_event` surface this PR establishes).

## Root Cause

N/A — feature PR. The lack of thinking visibility on subscription is a property of `claude -p` (headless) mode, not a regression in OpenClaw.

## Regression Test Plan

- **Target:** `extensions/anthropic/cli-backend-interactive.test.ts`.
- **Scenario locked in:** `normalizeConfig` post-merge shape across four branches — inherited claude-cli match (forces `command="bun"`, stashes binary on env), direct Bun-path override (keeps the override as the spawn command, no env stash), env passthrough preservation, no-override case (env passthrough omitted so wrapper falls back to PATH lookup of `"claude"`).
- **Why smallest reliable guardrail:** the failure mode is config-shape drift inside `normalizeConfig` — fully exercised synchronously without spawning bun or claude, runs in ms vs the minutes an e2e run would cost.

## Risks and Mitigations

- **Risk:** newly-installed user with no inherited `claude-cli` override could spawn `bun` as the claude binary if `normalizeConfig` stashed `merged.command` indiscriminately.
  - **Mitigation:** `normalizeConfig` consults `context.config.agents.defaults.cliBackends.claude-cli.command`; only stashes the env passthrough on a match. Regression test asserts the env var is `undefined` for the no-override branch.
- **Risk:** `tty-spoof.cjs` is a runtime support file (not a build output) and could be omitted from packaged installs.
  - **Mitigation:** declared in `extensions/anthropic/package.json#openclaw.build.staticAssets`; postbuild asset copier ships it alongside the wrapper.
- **Risk:** winget-installed `claude.exe` shadows the npm install via PATHEXT on Windows.
  - **Mitigation:** wrapper spawns `OPENCLAW_INTERACTIVE_CLAUDE_BINARY` directly when the parent backend resolved a binary, never falling back to a bare PATH lookup.
- **Risk:** title-gen / Haiku side-requests inject `{"title":"…"}` artifacts into the reasoning stream.
  - **Mitigation:** mitm-server classifies each `/v1/messages` request into `auxiliary` (no tools) / `compaction` (compact.ts prompt markers) / `tool_followup` (last msg has tool_result) / `normal`. Wrapper drops auxiliary entirely, streams compaction as `thinking_delta`, emits a result only on `normal` `end_turn`. Response-content fingerprint backup (`<analysis>` + Primary-Request-and-Intent + Pending-Tasks) catches anything that slipped past the request-level classifier.
- **Risk:** spilled-prompt overflow file leaks to concurrent runs via shared tempdir + `--add-dir`.
  - **Mitigation:** per-run subdir under `${tmpdir()}/openclaw-interactive-proxy/run-<32-hex-bytes>/` (0700), file 0600, `--add-dir` scoped to the per-run subdir only.
- **Risk:** local CA + leaf key persisted under user's OpenClaw config dir could be reused if an attacker gets read access.
  - **Mitigation:** trust scoped to the spawned claude child via `NODE_EXTRA_CA_CERTS` only (no system trust mutation); CA issues solely the one `api.anthropic.com` leaf the per-turn proxy needs; key files hardened to 0600 with fail-closed regeneration; maintainer security review invited.
- **Risk:** Anthropic-side non-SSE 4xx/5xx on `/v1/messages` (429, billing, overloaded) hangs the wrapper because claude doesn't self-exit on API error.
  - **Mitigation:** mitm-server emits a synthetic `api_error` event for non-SSE 4xx/5xx; wrapper surfaces a `result` with `is_error: true` and exits nonzero so the CLI runner treats the turn as failed and triggers failover.
- **Risk:** two resumes of the same Claude session race on the same transcript.
  - **Mitigation:** backend config sets `serialize: true` so the framework queues same-session runs; different sessions still run concurrently. mitm-server binds both proxy stages on port 0 so concurrent proxies can't collide on a preferred port either.

## Security Impact

- **New permissions/capabilities:** Yes — per-turn local TLS MITM on a loopback port. Scoped to the spawned claude child via `NODE_EXTRA_CA_CERTS`; system trust store untouched. Local CA + leaf key cached under user's OpenClaw config dir at 0700/0600.
- **Secrets/tokens handling changed:** No — wrapper does not read, log, or modify the Anthropic auth token; it is forwarded byte-for-byte to the re-issued upstream request.
- **New/changed network calls:** Yes — claude's outbound HTTPS to `api.anthropic.com:443` is rerouted via `HTTPS_PROXY=http://127.0.0.1:<port>` inside the child. Non–`api.anthropic.com` CONNECT tunnels (statsig, datadog, etc.) bypass the TLS terminator entirely.
- **Command/tool execution surface changed:** No — same `claude` binary, same allowed-tools (`mcp__openclaw__*`), same session semantics.
- **Data access scope changed:** No — proxy sees only the SSE bytes claude itself sees on the wire; nothing exfiltrated or aggregated outside the per-turn process.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible: existing `claude-cli` backend unchanged. Opt-in via the `claude-cli-interactive` id; no default flips.
- New optional env: `OPENCLAW_INTERACTIVE_PROXY_DEBUG=1` enables verbose wrapper stderr.
- Migration needed: no.

## Prior art

[`smithersai/claude-p`](https://github.com/smithersai/claude-p) runs `claude` without `-p` but bypasses the JSONL streaming surface entirely — works for the raw final text but loses the `stream_event` records that reasoning and tool-use rendering depend on. This PR reproduces the `-p --output-format stream-json` shape record-for-record from the SSE tap so existing OpenClaw stream consumers layer on unchanged.
